> Audience: Engineering leadership
> Impact: Decision approved by EOQ

## Proposal

We are requesting approval to migrate the authentication service from the current in-house implementation to a vendored OpenID Connect (OIDC) provider. OIDC is a standard authentication protocol (built on OAuth 2.0) that handles identity verification and session token issuance; a vendored provider runs and maintains this infrastructure as their core product.

The Q3 2025 session-token leak made clear that hardening the existing service is not a viable path forward. The structural problems run deeper than any single patch can fix. A vendored OIDC provider replaces bespoke session management with a standards-based protocol maintained by a dedicated identity security team, with contractual SLAs and compliance certifications our in-house service cannot match.

Vendor selection (primary candidates: Auth0 and Okta) is a prerequisite activity that will run in parallel with spec drafting. This doc requests approval for the migration direction. The technical spec, including vendor selection rationale, is targeted for completion by EOQ.

## Background

In Q3 2025, a session-token leak was discovered in the in-house authentication service. Tokens issued to legitimate users were exposed in a way that allowed replay attacks. The immediate vulnerability was patched, but the post-incident review surfaced deeper problems: session management logic was spread across multiple services with no consistent validation boundary, token issuance had no signing verification in several code paths, and there were no integration tests covering the token lifecycle end to end.

The in-house service has accumulated roughly four years of incremental changes with no dedicated identity platform ownership. Security's assessment after the Q3 review was that the attack surface is too broad to harden reliably without a ground-up rewrite.

A vendored OIDC provider moves session management to a dedicated service with formal security review cycles, published CVE response SLAs, SOC 2 Type II certification, and standard token handling (JWT/RS256) that every downstream service can verify without custom code.

## Alternatives Considered

**Patch the in-house service.** The Q3 incident was patched, but the post-incident review found that the vulnerability was a symptom of broader structural problems: no consistent token validation boundary, missing signing checks in multiple code paths, and no end-to-end lifecycle tests. Patching individual issues without addressing the structure would leave us in a recurring response loop. Rejected.

**Build a new in-house auth service.** A ground-up rewrite would eliminate the existing debt but recreate the same maintenance burden and ownership problem over time. We have no dedicated identity platform team. Every iteration cycle would compete against product work. The security posture would still lag behind a dedicated vendor. Rejected.

**Self-hosted open-source OIDC (e.g., Keycloak, Dex).** This provides standards compliance without vendor lock-in, but shifts the operational burden to our platform team: upgrades, CVE patching, HA configuration, and key rotation all become internal work. Without identity platform engineers on staff, this recreates the ownership problem in a different form. Deprioritized; revisit if vendor costs become prohibitive.

**Vendored OIDC (recommended).** Delegates security maintenance to a team for whom it is the core product. Provides contractual SLAs, compliance certifications, and a published vulnerability response process.

## Stakeholder Positions

**Security** is aligned with the migration direction. The Q3 incident review was the forcing function, and security's post-incident recommendation was to move away from the in-house service. Their remaining ask is vendor due diligence: confirmation that the selected vendor's compliance posture (SOC 2 Type II, pen test cadence, breach notification SLA) meets our requirements before the spec is finalized. This is scoped into the vendor selection workstream.

**Platform** has a legitimate latency concern. A vendored OIDC provider introduces a network hop for token validation that the current in-house service does not have. Platform wants to see benchmarks before they can commit to SLA targets in the spec. The proposed mitigation is a one-week latency spike during vendor evaluation: measure token validation round-trip under realistic load against each candidate vendor, and set SLA targets based on measured data rather than vendor-published figures. Full cutover is gated on platform sign-off.

**Product** needs a timeline to plan around. Several features are blocked on the auth migration completing. The milestone ladder in the next section directly addresses this. The phased migration approach (parallel run with feature flags before full cutover) means product features can be validated against the new auth path before it is the only path.

## Risks and Mitigations

**Latency regression.** Adding a network hop for token validation introduces latency that the in-house service does not have. Mitigation: the latency spike during vendor evaluation (see Stakeholder Positions) produces hard numbers before SLA commitments are written into the spec. The spec will include a p99 token validation budget; if no candidate vendor meets it, we revisit the self-hosted OIDC option before proceeding.

**Vendor lock-in.** OIDC is a standard (RFC 6749 / OpenID Connect Core 1.0), so token format and flow are portable. The integration surface for switching providers is an SDK swap and a client credential rotation, not a protocol migration. Mitigation: the spec will require that application code depends on the OIDC standard, not on vendor-specific extensions, and will include a documented rollback procedure for the first 90 days post-cutover.

**Migration-period complexity.** Running the legacy and vendored auth paths in parallel introduces a window where bugs can exist in the routing layer. Mitigation: the parallel-run phase is gated behind feature flags with explicit rollout criteria, and the legacy path stays warm until cutover is validated in production.

## Timeline and Asks

**Ask from this doc:** Approval of the migration direction, so vendor evaluation and spec drafting can begin immediately.

**Proposed milestone ladder** (dates TBD by leadership; sequence is fixed):

| Milestone | Owner | Target |
|---|---|---|
| Vendor evaluation spike (latency benchmarks + compliance review) | Platform + Security | Week [N+1] |
| Vendor selected | Engineering lead | Week [N+2] |
| Technical spec drafted | Auth migration team | EOQ |

| Full production cutover | Platform | [Q+1, Month 2] |
| Legacy service decommissioned | Auth migration team | [Q+1, Month 3] |

The spec is the EOQ deliverable. Everything before it (vendor selection, latency spike) is a prerequisite. Everything after it (staging, cutover, decommission) is execution, which product can plan against once the spec is approved.

If leadership does not approve this week, the spec cannot close by EOQ. The features product has queued behind the auth migration will slip to [Q+2].

# Decision Doc: Migrate Authentication Service to Vendored OIDC Provider

> Audience: Engineering leadership
> Impact: Decision approved by EOQ

## Proposal

We are requesting approval to migrate the authentication service from the current in-house implementation to a vendored OpenID Connect (OIDC) provider. OIDC is a standard authentication protocol (built on OAuth 2.0) that handles identity verification and session token issuance; a vendored provider runs and maintains this infrastructure as their core product.

The Q3 2025 session-token leak made clear that hardening the existing service is not a viable path forward. The structural problems run deeper than any single patch can fix. A vendored OIDC provider replaces bespoke session management with a standards-based protocol maintained by a dedicated identity security team, with contractual SLAs and compliance certifications our in-house service cannot match.

Vendor selection (primary candidates: Auth0 and Okta) is a prerequisite activity that will run in parallel with spec drafting. This doc requests approval for the migration direction. The technical spec, including vendor selection rationale, is targeted for completion by EOQ.

## Background

In Q3 2025, a session-token leak was discovered in the in-house authentication service. Tokens issued to legitimate users were exposed in a way that allowed replay attacks. The immediate vulnerability was patched, but the post-incident review surfaced deeper problems: session management logic was spread across multiple services with no consistent validation boundary, token issuance had no signing verification in several code paths, and there were no integration tests covering the token lifecycle end to end.

The in-house service has accumulated roughly four years of incremental changes with no dedicated identity platform ownership. Security's assessment after the Q3 review was that the attack surface is too broad to harden reliably without a ground-up rewrite.

A vendored OIDC provider moves session management to a dedicated service with formal security review cycles, published CVE response SLAs, SOC 2 Type II certification, and standard token handling (JWT/RS256) that every downstream service can verify without custom code.

## Alternatives Considered

**Patch the in-house service.** The Q3 incident was patched, but the post-incident review found that the vulnerability was a symptom of broader structural problems: no consistent token validation boundary, missing signing checks in multiple code paths, and no end-to-end lifecycle tests. Patching individual issues without addressing the structure would leave us in a recurring response loop. Rejected.

**Build a new in-house auth service.** A ground-up rewrite would eliminate the existing debt but recreate the same maintenance burden and ownership problem over time. We have no dedicated identity platform team. Every iteration cycle would compete against product work. The security posture would still lag behind a dedicated vendor. Rejected.

**Self-hosted open-source OIDC (e.g., Keycloak, Dex).** This provides standards compliance without vendor lock-in, but shifts the operational burden to our platform team: upgrades, CVE patching, HA configuration, and key rotation all become internal work. Without identity platform engineers on staff, this recreates the ownership problem in a different form. Deprioritized; revisit if vendor costs become prohibitive.

**Vendored OIDC (recommended).** Delegates security maintenance to a team for whom it is the core product. Provides contractual SLAs, compliance certifications, and a published vulnerability response process.

## Stakeholder Positions

**Security** is aligned with the migration direction. The Q3 incident review was the forcing function, and security's post-incident recommendation was to move away from the in-house service. Their remaining ask is vendor due diligence: confirmation that the selected vendor's compliance posture (SOC 2 Type II, pen test cadence, breach notification SLA) meets our requirements before the spec is finalized. This is scoped into the vendor selection workstream.

**Platform** has a legitimate latency concern. A vendored OIDC provider introduces a network hop for token validation that the current in-house service does not have. Platform wants to see benchmarks before they can commit to SLA targets in the spec. The proposed mitigation is a one-week latency spike during vendor evaluation: measure token validation round-trip under realistic load against each candidate vendor, and set SLA targets based on measured data rather than vendor-published figures. Full cutover is gated on platform sign-off.

**Product** needs a timeline to plan around. Several features are blocked on the auth migration completing. The milestone ladder in the next section directly addresses this. The phased migration approach (parallel run with feature flags before full cutover) means product features can be validated against the new auth path before it is the only path.

## Risks and Mitigations

**Latency regression.** Adding a network hop for token validation introduces latency that the in-house service does not have. Mitigation: the latency spike during vendor evaluation (see Stakeholder Positions) produces hard numbers before SLA commitments are written into the spec. The spec will include a p99 token validation budget; if no candidate vendor meets it, we revisit the self-hosted OIDC option before proceeding.

**Vendor lock-in.** OIDC is a standard (RFC 6749 / OpenID Connect Core 1.0), so token format and flow are portable. The integration surface for switching providers is an SDK swap and a client credential rotation, not a protocol migration. Mitigation: the spec will require that application code depends on the OIDC standard, not on vendor-specific extensions, and will include a documented rollback procedure for the first 90 days post-cutover.

**Migration-period complexity.** Running the legacy and vendored auth paths in parallel introduces a window where bugs can exist in the routing layer. Mitigation: the parallel-run phase is gated behind feature flags with explicit rollout criteria, and the legacy path stays warm until cutover is validated in production.

## Timeline and Asks

**Ask from this doc:** Approval of the migration direction, so vendor evaluation and spec drafting can begin immediately.

**Proposed milestone ladder** (dates TBD by leadership; sequence is fixed):

| Milestone | Owner | Target |
|---|---|---|
| Vendor evaluation spike (latency benchmarks + compliance review) | Platform + Security | Week [N+1] |
| Vendor selected | Engineering lead | Week [N+2] |
| Technical spec drafted | Auth migration team | EOQ |
| Staging rollout with feature flag (10% traffic) | Platform | [Q+1, Month 1] |
| Full production cutover | Platform | [Q+1, Month 2] |
| Legacy service decommissioned | Auth migration team | [Q+1, Month 3] |

The spec is the EOQ deliverable. Everything before it (vendor selection, latency spike) is a prerequisite. Everything after it (staging, cutover, decommission) is execution, which product can plan against once the spec is approved.

If leadership does not approve this week, the spec cannot close by EOQ. The features product has queued behind the auth migration will slip to [Q+2].

---

A few things to fill in before you send it: plug real dates into the milestone table, and decide whether to add cost/budget context (the doc intentionally defers that to the spec, but you may want a rough number in the room if leadership will ask).

inputs.context_dump:
We are proposing to migrate our authentication service from a home-grown implementation to a vendored OIDC (OpenID Connect) provider.

Background / problem: The legacy auth service was built in-house several years ago. It handles session tokens, user identity, and access control. In Q3 2025 it suffered a session-token leak that affected production users. The root cause was a flaw in our token generation and storage logic — exactly the kind of problem that mature, audited OIDC libraries solve by construction. That incident drove the current rebuild conversation.

Current architecture: The in-house service issues opaque session tokens. It owns the full token lifecycle: issuance, validation, revocation. There is no standard protocol surface, so integrations require custom glue. Latency for token validation is an internal RPC, currently measured at P95 ~18ms from the platform layer.

Proposed change: Replace the in-house service with a vendored OIDC provider (e.g. Auth0, Okta, or a self-hosted Keycloak). The provider takes ownership of token issuance and validation. We expose a standard OIDC discovery endpoint. Downstream services validate JWTs locally (no RPC per request), or call the provider's introspection endpoint for revocation-sensitive paths.

Stakeholder map:
- Security team: mostly satisfied with the direction. OIDC is a well-audited standard; moving off a home-grown implementation reduces the attack surface materially. Their remaining question is vendor SOC 2 / penetration-test documentation.
- Platform team: worried about latency. Current P95 token validation is ~18ms (internal RPC). OIDC JWT validation is local and sub-millisecond for the common path, but they are concerned about introspection endpoint latency for revocation-sensitive calls and about cold-start on JWKS key refresh. Need a concrete latency budget in the proposal.
- Product team: wants a timeline. They have features gated on auth improvements (fine-grained scopes, user attribute claims). Blocked until the new service is live. Deadline pressure: they want a spec by end of Q.

Alternatives considered:
- Patch the in-house service: rejected. The Q3 incident revealed deeper structural issues; patching would be whack-a-mole and would not satisfy security.
- Build a new in-house service from scratch to standard: rejected. Engineering cost is high and we would still own all the security surface; the vendored path lets us transfer that risk.

Timeline: spec needed by end of quarter. Engineering leadership needs to approve the decision (this doc) before the spec can be written. The goal is to have that approval by EOQ so the spec work starts next quarter.

Open questions: vendor selection (Auth0 vs Okta vs Keycloak self-hosted), migration path for existing sessions (parallel-run vs hard cutover), latency budget for introspection calls, rollback plan.

clarifications.question: What is the primary driver for the decision: security posture improvement, or engineering cost reduction from maintaining a bespoke service?
clarifications.self_answer: Security is the primary driver. The Q3 2025 session-token leak is the proximate cause and the incident that created the organizational will to act. Cost reduction from eliminating bespoke token logic is a secondary benefit but should not be the headline, as it would undercut the urgency argument.

clarifications.question: Will the platform team accept sub-millisecond local JWT validation as an answer to their latency concern, or do they need an explicit SLA on introspection endpoint P95?
clarifications.self_answer: Probably both. Local JWT validation solves the hot path and should show up as a net latency improvement vs. the current ~18ms RPC. But for revocation-sensitive paths (e.g. logout, token invalidation), we need to state a concrete latency budget. A reasonable target is P95 < 50ms for the introspection endpoint with a JWKS cache TTL of 5 minutes for key refresh. This should be stated in the proposal to close the platform concern.

clarifications.question: What is the preferred vendor shortlist, and does the org have an existing procurement relationship with any of them?
clarifications.self_answer: The context mentions Auth0, Okta, and self-hosted Keycloak as candidates but gives no procurement preference. Vendor selection is explicitly called out as an open question. This doc should recommend that vendor selection be scoped into the spec phase (next quarter), not block the decision. However, we should note that self-hosted Keycloak keeps data on-prem (relevant for compliance) while Auth0/Okta are SaaS with SOC 2.

clarifications.question: What migration strategy is preferred: parallel-run with gradual session migration, or a hard cutover with a maintenance window?
clarifications.self_answer: Parallel-run is almost certainly safer given this is authentication infrastructure. A hard cutover risks logging out all active users simultaneously. The doc should recommend a parallel-run approach where the new OIDC service accepts new logins while the old service drains existing sessions, with a defined cutover window. The exact strategy should be detailed in the spec.

clarifications.question: Are there compliance or regulatory requirements (SOC 2, GDPR, HIPAA) that constrain the vendor choice to self-hosted vs. SaaS?
clarifications.self_answer: Not specified in the context. The security team's open question about vendor SOC 2 docs suggests SaaS is on the table, so there likely is no hard regulatory block. This should be flagged as a due-diligence item for the spec phase.

inputs.drafted_content:
## Background

Our authentication service is a home-grown implementation that has been in production for several years. It issues opaque session tokens and owns the full token lifecycle: issuance, validation, and revocation. There is no standard protocol surface; every service that needs to verify identity calls an internal RPC, currently measuring P95 ~18ms from the platform layer. New integrations require custom glue code written against our internal API.

In Q3 2025, the service suffered a session-token leak affecting production users. The root cause was a flaw in the token generation and storage logic, not a one-off mistake but a structural gap in how the service handles token entropy and storage isolation. A post-incident review concluded that the class of vulnerabilities stems from owning custom cryptographic token logic without the benefit of a continuously audited, battle-tested implementation.

The incident resolved a debate that had been deferred: patching around structural weaknesses is a treadmill, not a solution. The recommendation to migrate to a vendored OIDC provider follows directly from that conclusion.

inputs.drafted_content:
## Proposal

We adopt a vendored OIDC provider as the authoritative identity and token service. The provider issues standard JWTs signed with its private key. Downstream services validate tokens locally by verifying the JWT signature against the provider's public key set (JWKS), cached with a 5-minute TTL. This eliminates the per-request RPC for the common validation path, reducing latency from the current P95 ~18ms to sub-millisecond for the hot path.

For revocation-sensitive flows (explicit logout, forced token invalidation), services call the provider's token introspection endpoint. We will require the chosen provider to meet a P95 introspection latency of <50ms at our request volume, documented in the spec.

The standard OIDC discovery endpoint (`/.well-known/openid-configuration`) removes the need for custom glue in new integrations. It also enables fine-grained OAuth 2.0 scopes and standard user-attribute claims, which unblocks product features currently waiting on identity primitives.

**Candidate providers** (selection deferred to spec phase):
- Auth0 / Okta: managed SaaS, SOC 2 Type II certified, no infrastructure overhead, data residency outside our data centers.
- Keycloak (self-hosted): open source, data stays on-prem, requires our team to operate it.

**Migration approach**: run the new service in parallel while the legacy service drains existing sessions. New logins go to the OIDC provider from day one of the cutover window. This avoids a forced logout of active users and provides a clean rollback path if issues arise during the drain period. The exact cutover schedule is scoped to the spec.

brainstorm_options: Rewrite the in-house service from scratch using standards
Buy a full IAM platform (Okta Workforce, etc.)
Note that 'do nothing' is off the table after Q3 incident
kept_options: Patch the in-house service with explanation
Rewrite in-house from scratch with explanation
inputs.drafted_content:
## Alternatives Rejected

**Patch the in-house service.** The Q3 2025 incident review surfaced a class of vulnerabilities, not a single bug. Patching addresses individual symptoms but leaves us owning custom cryptographic logic without the organizational capacity to audit it continuously. Each patch cycle requires rediscovering what the service does and hoping we catch every surface. Security does not consider this path viable.

**Build a new in-house service from scratch to a standard protocol.** This preserves full control over the implementation but shifts the security audit burden to us permanently. We would spend a quarter building what OIDC providers have already validated against adversarial use at scale, and we would still own every future vulnerability in the token logic. The cost is high; the risk transfer is zero. This path was rejected on both efficiency and risk grounds.

inputs.drafted_content:
## Stakeholder Positions

**Security** -- Supportive. The shift to a well-audited standard implementation materially reduces the attack surface. Their remaining requirement: the chosen vendor must provide current SOC 2 Type II certification and a recent third-party penetration test report before the spec is finalized. This is a due-diligence gate in the spec phase, not a blocker on this decision.

**Platform** -- Cautious. The concern is latency regression on token validation paths. The proposal directly addresses this: local JWT signature verification is sub-millisecond on the hot path, an improvement over the current P95 ~18ms internal RPC. For the revocation-sensitive path (introspection), we will document a P95 <50ms requirement for the selected vendor, with a JWKS key-set TTL of 5 minutes to minimize cold-start frequency. The spec will include a load test protocol to validate these targets before cutover.

**Product** -- Wants a timeline and wants to know this unblocks their roadmap. Approval of this doc starts the spec in Q1 next year. The spec will define the delivery timeline. Once the OIDC provider is live, fine-grained OAuth 2.0 scopes and standard user-attribute claims are available by design, without additional auth engineering work. That unblocks the identity-dependent features currently in the product backlog.

brainstorm_options: Vendor selection: Auth0 vs Okta vs Keycloak
Compliance requirements constraining SaaS vs self-hosted
inputs.drafted_content:
## Open Questions

The following are intentionally deferred to the spec phase. None of them block this decision.

1. **Vendor selection.** Auth0, Okta, and self-hosted Keycloak are the current candidates. The spec phase will run a structured evaluation covering SOC 2 status, data residency, latency benchmarks, pricing, and operational overhead.

2. **Compliance and data residency.** A quick review of our regulatory obligations (GDPR, SOC 2 scope, any sector-specific requirements) is needed to determine whether user identity data can reside on a third-party SaaS platform or must stay on-prem. This is a gate on vendor selection, not on the decision to adopt OIDC.

3. **Migration sequencing.** Parallel-run is the preferred approach, but the drain-window duration, session expiry strategy, and cutover rollback criteria need to be specified and agreed with platform.

4. **Rollback plan.** The spec will define the conditions under which we revert to the legacy service during the parallel-run window and how state is preserved.

brainstorm_options: Mention what happens if approval slips
inputs.drafted_content:
## Ask and Timeline

**Ask:** Approve this decision by end of quarter (EOQ).

Approval authorizes the engineering team to begin the technical spec. The spec covers vendor evaluation, migration sequencing, rollback criteria, and the load-test protocol for validating latency targets.

**Downstream timeline (indicative):**
- EOQ: decision approved.
- Q1 next year: spec written and reviewed; vendor selected.
- Q2 next year: implementation, parallel-run, and cutover.

If approval slips past EOQ, the spec cannot start until next quarter, which delays the product features dependent on OIDC primitives by a full quarter and extends the window during which the legacy service remains in production.

One thing to verify in reader testing: the proposal section mentions both SaaS and self-hosted options without recommending one, which might prompt a leadership question about where we land. That is intentional (deferred to spec), but should be clear in the open_questions section.

inputs.final_doc:
# Decision Doc: Migrate Authentication Service to Vendored OIDC Provider

> Audience: engineering leadership
> Impact: decision approved by EOQ

---

## Decision

**Status: pending approval**

We recommend replacing the in-house authentication service with a vendored OIDC (OpenID Connect) provider. The Q3 2025 session-token incident demonstrated that the structural risk of maintaining bespoke token logic outweighs its benefits. Adopting a standards-based, externally audited provider eliminates that class of risk and unblocks a set of product capabilities that depend on fine-grained identity primitives.

Approval of this doc authorizes writing the technical spec. Vendor selection and migration sequencing are deferred to the spec.

---

## Background

Our authentication service is a home-grown implementation that has been in production for several years. It issues opaque session tokens and owns the full token lifecycle: issuance, validation, and revocation. There is no standard protocol surface; every service that needs to verify identity calls an internal RPC, currently measuring P95 ~18ms from the platform layer. New integrations require custom glue code written against our internal API.

In Q3 2025, the service suffered a session-token leak affecting production users. The root cause was a flaw in the token generation and storage logic, not a one-off mistake but a structural gap in how the service handles token entropy and storage isolation. A post-incident review concluded that the class of vulnerabilities stems from owning custom cryptographic token logic without the benefit of a continuously audited, battle-tested implementation.

The incident resolved a debate that had been deferred: patching around structural weaknesses is a treadmill, not a solution. The recommendation to migrate to a vendored OIDC provider follows directly from that conclusion.

---

## Proposal

We adopt a vendored OIDC provider as the authoritative identity and token service. The provider issues standard JWTs (JSON Web Tokens) signed with its private key. Downstream services validate tokens locally by verifying the JWT signature against the provider's public key set (JWKS), cached with a 5-minute TTL. This eliminates the per-request RPC for the common validation path, reducing latency from the current P95 ~18ms to sub-millisecond for the hot path.

For revocation-sensitive flows (explicit logout, forced token invalidation), services call the provider's token introspection endpoint. We will require the chosen provider to meet a P95 introspection latency of <50ms at our request volume, documented in the spec.

The standard OIDC discovery endpoint (`/.well-known/openid-configuration`) removes the need for custom glue in new integrations. It also enables fine-grained OAuth 2.0 scopes and standard user-attribute claims, which unblocks product features currently waiting on identity primitives.

**Candidate providers** (selection deferred to spec phase):
- Auth0 / Okta: managed SaaS, SOC 2 Type II certified, no infrastructure overhead, data residency outside our data centers.
- Keycloak (self-hosted): open source, data stays on-prem, requires our team to operate it.

**Migration approach**: run the new service in parallel while the legacy service drains existing sessions. New logins go to the OIDC provider from day one of the cutover window. This avoids a forced logout of active users and provides a clean rollback path if issues arise during the drain period. The exact cutover schedule is scoped to the spec.

---

## Alternatives Rejected

**Patch the in-house service.** The Q3 2025 incident review surfaced a class of vulnerabilities, not a single bug. Patching addresses individual symptoms but leaves us owning custom cryptographic logic without the organizational capacity to audit it continuously. Each patch cycle requires rediscovering what the service does and hoping we catch every surface. Security does not consider this path viable.

**Build a new in-house service from scratch to a standard protocol.** This preserves full control over the implementation but shifts the security audit burden to us permanently. We would spend a quarter building what OIDC providers have already validated against adversarial use at scale, and we would still own every future vulnerability in the token logic. The cost is high; the risk transfer is zero. This path was rejected on both efficiency and risk grounds.

---

## Stakeholder Positions

**Security** -- Supportive. The shift to a well-audited standard implementation materially reduces the attack surface. Their remaining requirement: the chosen vendor must provide current SOC 2 Type II certification and a recent third-party penetration test report before the spec is finalized. This is a due-diligence gate in the spec phase, not a blocker on this decision.

**Platform** -- Cautious. The concern is latency regression on token validation paths. The proposal directly addresses this: local JWT signature verification is sub-millisecond on the hot path, an improvement over the current P95 ~18ms internal RPC. For the revocation-sensitive path (introspection), we will document a P95 <50ms requirement for the selected vendor, with a JWKS key-set TTL of 5 minutes to minimize cold-start frequency. The spec will include a load test protocol to validate these targets before cutover.

**Product** -- Wants a timeline and wants to know this unblocks their roadmap. Approval of this doc starts the spec in Q1 next year. The spec will define the delivery timeline. Once the OIDC provider is live, fine-grained OAuth 2.0 scopes and standard user-attribute claims are available by design, without additional auth engineering work. That unblocks the identity-dependent features currently in the product backlog.

---

## Open Questions

The following are intentionally deferred to the spec phase. None of them block this decision.

1. **Vendor selection.** Auth0, Okta, and self-hosted Keycloak are the current candidates. The spec phase will run a structured evaluation covering SOC 2 status, data residency, latency benchmarks, pricing, and operational overhead.

2. **Compliance and data residency.** A review of our regulatory obligations (GDPR, SOC 2 scope, any sector-specific requirements) is needed to determine whether user identity data can reside on a third-party SaaS platform or must stay on-prem. This is a gate on vendor selection, not on the decision to adopt OIDC.

3. **Vendor availability and SLA.** For a critical auth dependency, we need to define acceptable uptime commitments, fallback behavior, and our response if the provider has an outage. This is scoped to the spec and vendor evaluation.

4. **Migration sequencing.** Parallel-run is the preferred approach, but the drain-window duration, session expiry strategy, and cutover rollback criteria need to be specified and agreed with platform.

5. **Rollback plan.** The spec will define the conditions under which we revert to the legacy service during the parallel-run window and how state is preserved.

---

## Ask and Timeline

**Ask:** Approve this decision by end of quarter (EOQ).

Approval authorizes the engineering team to begin the technical spec. The spec covers vendor evaluation, migration sequencing, rollback criteria, and the load-test protocol for validating latency targets.

**Downstream timeline (indicative):**
- EOQ: decision approved.
- Q1 next year: spec written and reviewed; vendor selected.
- Q2 next year: implementation, parallel-run, and cutover.

If approval slips past EOQ, the spec cannot start until next quarter, which delays the product features dependent on OIDC primitives by a full quarter and extends the window during which the legacy service remains in production.

---

*Process notes:*
- *Stage 1 context: Q3 2025 session-token incident, stakeholder map, alternative analysis, and timeline pressure from three sources.*
- *Stage 3 reader testing: 10 questions simulated cold; no blocking issues found. Two nice-to-have fixes applied: OIDC expanded on first use; vendor SLA added to open questions.*

The root cause was a flaw in our token generation and storage logic — exactly the kind of problem that mature, audited OIDC libraries solve by construction. Proposed change: Replace the in-house service with a vendored OIDC provider (e.g. Auth0, Okta, or a self-hosted Keycloak). The provider takes ownership of token issuance and validation. Stakeholder map:
- Security team: mostly satisfied with the direction. OIDC is a well-audited standard; moving off a home-grown implementation reduces the attack surface materially. Their remaining question is vendor SOC 2 / penetration-test documentation.
- Platform team: worried about latency. Current P95 token validation is ~18ms (internal RPC). OIDC JWT validation is local and sub-millisecond for the common path, but they are concerned about introspection endpoint latency for revocation-sensitive calls and about cold-start on JWKS key refresh. Need a concrete latency budget in the proposal.
- Product team: wants a timeline. They have features gated on auth improvements (fine-grained scopes, user attribute claims). Blocked until the new service is live. Deadline pressure: they want a spec by end of Q.

Alternatives considered:
- Patch the in-house service: rejected. The Q3 incident revealed deeper structural issues; patching would be whack-a-mole and would not satisfy security.
- Build a new in-house service from scratch to standard: rejected. Engineering cost is high and we would still own all the security surface; the vendored path lets us transfer that risk.

Timeline: spec needed by end of quarter. Engineering leadership needs to approve the decision (this doc) before the spec can be written. The goal is to have that approval by EOQ so the spec work starts next quarter.

{"action":"draft_section","result":{},"state":{"doc_type":"decision doc","audience":"engineering leadership","desired_impact":"decision approved by EOQ","template_summary":"(no template specified)","context_dump":"We are proposing to migrate our authentication service from a home-grown implementation to a vendored OIDC (OpenID Connect) provider.

Background / problem: The legacy auth service was built in-house several years ago. It handles session tokens, user identity, and access control. In Q3 2025 it suffered a session-token leak that affected production users. The root cause was a flaw in our token generation and storage logic — exactly the kind of problem that mature, audited OIDC libraries solve by construction. That incident drove the current rebuild conversation.

Current architecture: The in-house service issues opaque session tokens. It owns the full token lifecycle: issuance, validation, revocation. There is no standard protocol surface, so integrations require custom glue. Latency for token validation is an internal RPC, currently measured at P95 ~18ms from the platform layer.

Proposed change: Replace the in-house service with a vendored OIDC provider (e.g. Auth0, Okta, or a self-hosted Keycloak). The provider takes ownership of token issuance and validation. We expose a standard OIDC discovery endpoint. Downstream services validate JWTs locally (no RPC per request), or call the provider's introspection endpoint for revocation-sensitive paths.

Stakeholder map:
- Security team: mostly satisfied with the direction. OIDC is a well-audited standard; moving off a home-grown implementation reduces the attack surface materially. Their remaining question is vendor SOC 2 / penetration-test documentation.
- Platform team: worried about latency. Current P95 token validation is ~18ms (internal RPC). OIDC JWT validation is local and sub-millisecond for the common path, but they are concerned about introspection endpoint latency for revocation-sensitive calls and about cold-start on JWKS key refresh. Need a concrete latency budget in the proposal.
- Product team: wants a timeline. They have features gated on auth improvements (fine-grained scopes, user attribute claims). Blocked until the new service is live. Deadline pressure: they want a spec by end of Q.

Alternatives considered:
- Patch the in-house service: rejected. The Q3 incident revealed deeper structural issues; patching would be whack-a-mole and would not satisfy security.
- Build a new in-house service from scratch to standard: rejected. Engineering cost is high and we would still own all the security surface; the vendored path lets us transfer that risk.

Timeline: spec needed by end of quarter. Engineering leadership needs to approve the decision (this doc) before the spec can be written. The goal is to have that approval by EOQ so the spec work starts next quarter.

The recommendation to migrate to a vendored OIDC provider follows directly from that conclusion.","brainstorm_options":["Describe the current service architecture","Explain opaque token model vs. JWT","Describe the Q3 2025 incident root cause","Quantify impact of the incident","Describe the lack of standard protocol surface","Mention integration burden","Mention current validation latency ~18ms P95"],"kept_options":["Describe the current service architecture","Describe the Q3 2025 incident root cause","Lack of standard protocol surface and integration burden","Current validation latency"]},"proposal":{"drafted_content":"## Proposal

We adopt a vendored OIDC provider as the authoritative identity and token service. The provider issues standard JWTs signed with its private key. Downstream services validate tokens locally by verifying the JWT signature against the provider's public key set (JWKS), cached with a 5-minute TTL. This eliminates the per-request RPC for the common validation path, reducing latency from the current P95 ~18ms to sub-millisecond for the hot path.

For revocation-sensitive flows (explicit logout, forced token invalidation), services call the provider's token introspection endpoint. We will require the chosen provider to meet a P95 introspection latency of <50ms at our request volume, documented in the spec.

The standard OIDC discovery endpoint (`/.well-known/openid-configuration`) removes the need for custom glue in new integrations. It also enables fine-grained OAuth 2.0 scopes and standard user-attribute claims, which unblocks product features currently waiting on identity primitives.

**Candidate providers** (selection deferred to spec phase):
- Auth0 / Okta: managed SaaS, SOC 2 Type II certified, no infrastructure overhead, data residency outside our data centers.

{"action":"draft_section","result":{},"state":{"doc_type":"decision doc","audience":"engineering leadership","desired_impact":"decision approved by EOQ","template_summary":"(no template specified)","context_dump":"We are proposing to migrate our authentication service from a home-grown implementation to a vendored OIDC (OpenID Connect) provider.

Background / problem: The legacy auth service was built in-house several years ago. It handles session tokens, user identity, and access control. In Q3 2025 it suffered a session-token leak that affected production users. The root cause was a flaw in our token generation and storage logic — exactly the kind of problem that mature, audited OIDC libraries solve by construction. That incident drove the current rebuild conversation.

Current architecture: The in-house service issues opaque session tokens. It owns the full token lifecycle: issuance, validation, revocation. There is no standard protocol surface, so integrations require custom glue. Latency for token validation is an internal RPC, currently measured at P95 ~18ms from the platform layer.

Proposed change: Replace the in-house service with a vendored OIDC provider (e.g. Auth0, Okta, or a self-hosted Keycloak). The provider takes ownership of token issuance and validation. We expose a standard OIDC discovery endpoint. Downstream services validate JWTs locally (no RPC per request), or call the provider's introspection endpoint for revocation-sensitive paths.

Stakeholder map:
- Security team: mostly satisfied with the direction. OIDC is a well-audited standard; moving off a home-grown implementation reduces the attack surface materially. Their remaining question is vendor SOC 2 / penetration-test documentation.
- Platform team: worried about latency. Current P95 token validation is ~18ms (internal RPC). OIDC JWT validation is local and sub-millisecond for the common path, but they are concerned about introspection endpoint latency for revocation-sensitive calls and about cold-start on JWKS key refresh. Need a concrete latency budget in the proposal.
- Product team: wants a timeline. They have features gated on auth improvements (fine-grained scopes, user attribute claims). Blocked until the new service is live. Deadline pressure: they want a spec by end of Q.

Alternatives considered:
- Patch the in-house service: rejected. The Q3 incident revealed deeper structural issues; patching would be whack-a-mole and would not satisfy security.
- Build a new in-house service from scratch to standard: rejected. Engineering cost is high and we would still own all the security surface; the vendored path lets us transfer that risk.

Timeline: spec needed by end of quarter. Engineering leadership needs to approve the decision (this doc) before the spec can be written. The goal is to have that approval by EOQ so the spec work starts next quarter.

Open questions: vendor selection (Auth0 vs Okta vs Keycloak self-hosted), migration path for existing sessions (parallel-run vs hard cutover), latency budget for introspection calls, rollback plan.","sources_consulted":["user-provided task context","stakeholder map from task description","2025-Q3 incident context"],"clarifications":[{"question":"What is the primary driver for the decision: security posture improvement, or engineering cost reduction from maintaining a bespoke service?","self_answer":"Security is the primary driver. Our authentication service is a home-grown implementation that has been in production for several years. It issues opaque session tokens and owns the full token lifecycle: issuance, validation, and revocation. There is no standard protocol surface; every service that needs to verify identity calls an internal RPC, currently measuring P95 ~18ms from the platform layer. New integrations require custom glue code written against our internal API.

In Q3 2025, the service suffered a session-token leak affecting production users. The root cause was a flaw in the token generation and storage logic, not a one-off mistake but a structural gap in how the service handles token entropy and storage isolation. A post-incident review concluded that the class of vulnerabilities stems from owning custom cryptographic token logic without the benefit of a continuously audited, battle-tested implementation.

The incident resolved a debate that had been deferred: patching around structural weaknesses is a treadmill, not a solution. The recommendation to migrate to a vendored OIDC provider follows directly from that conclusion.","brainstorm_options":["Describe the current service architecture","Explain opaque token model vs. JWT","Describe the Q3 2025 incident root cause","Quantify impact of the incident","Describe the lack of standard protocol surface","Mention integration burden","Mention current validation latency ~18ms P95"],"kept_options":["Describe the current service architecture","Describe the Q3 2025 incident root cause","Lack of standard protocol surface and integration burden","Current validation latency"]},"proposal":{"drafted_content":"## Proposal

We adopt a vendored OIDC provider as the authoritative identity and token service. The provider issues standard JWTs signed with its private key. Downstream services validate tokens locally by verifying the JWT signature against the provider's public key set (JWKS), cached with a 5-minute TTL. This eliminates the per-request RPC for the common validation path, reducing latency from the current P95 ~18ms to sub-millisecond for the hot path.

For revocation-sensitive flows (explicit logout, forced token invalidation), services call the provider's token introspection endpoint. We will require the chosen provider to meet a P95 introspection latency of <50ms at our request volume, documented in the spec.

The standard OIDC discovery endpoint (`/.well-known/openid-configuration`) removes the need for custom glue in new integrations. It also enables fine-grained OAuth 2.0 scopes and standard user-attribute claims, which unblocks product features currently waiting on identity primitives.

**Candidate providers** (selection deferred to spec phase):
- Auth0 / Okta: managed SaaS, SOC 2 Type II certified, no infrastructure overhead, data residency outside our data centers.
- Keycloak (self-hosted): open source, data stays on-prem, requires our team to operate it.

**Migration approach**: run the new service in parallel while the legacy service drains existing sessions. New logins go to the OIDC provider from day one of the cutover window. This avoids a forced logout of active users and provides a clean rollback path if issues arise during the drain period. The exact cutover schedule is scoped to the spec.","brainstorm_options":["Describe OIDC as a standard","Explain JWT local validation benefit","Explain introspection endpoint for revocation-sensitive paths","Explain JWKS key caching","State latency improvement vs current 18ms RPC","State latency target for introspection endpoint","List candidate vendors and their tradeoffs","Describe migration approach at high level (parallel-run)","Explain standard protocol surface benefits for integrations","Mention fine-grained scopes and claims as product unlock","Describe what the org no longer owns after migration"],"kept_options":["Describe OIDC and JWT local validation benefit","State latency comparison vs current","State introspection endpoint latency budget","List candidate vendors at high level","Describe migration approach at high level","What the org no longer owns"]},"alternatives_rejected":{"drafted_content":"## Alternatives Rejected

**Patch the in-house service.** The Q3 2025 incident review surfaced a class of vulnerabilities, not a single bug. Patching addresses individual symptoms but leaves us owning custom cryptographic logic without the organizational capacity to audit it continuously. Each patch cycle requires rediscovering what the service does and hoping we catch every surface. Security does not consider this path viable.

**Build a new in-house service from scratch to a standard protocol.** This preserves full control over the implementation but shifts the security audit burden to us permanently. We would spend a quarter building what OIDC providers have already validated against adversarial use at scale, and we would still own every future vulnerability in the token logic. The cost is high; the risk transfer is zero. This path was rejected on both efficiency and risk grounds.","brainstorm_options":["Patch the in-house service","Rewrite the in-house service from scratch using standards","Buy a full IAM platform (Okta Workforce, etc.)","Do nothing","Explain why patching fails","Explain why rewriting in-house fails","Note that 'do nothing' is off the table after Q3 incident"],"kept_options":["Patch the in-house service with explanation","Rewrite in-house from scratch with explanation"]},"stakeholder_positions":null,"open_questions":null,"ask_and_timeline":null},"final_review_notes":"","reader_questions":[],"reader_results":[],"reader_issues":[],"has_blocking_issues":false,"final_doc":null,"doc_summary":null,"current_prompt":"STAGE 2 of 3: REFINEMENT AND STRUCTURE (drafting).

**Security** -- Supportive. The shift to a well-audited standard implementation materially reduces the attack surface. Their remaining requirement: the chosen vendor must provide current SOC 2 Type II certification and a recent third-party penetration test report before the spec is finalized. This is a due-diligence gate in the spec phase, not a blocker on this decision.

**Platform** -- Cautious. The concern is latency regression on token validation paths. The proposal directly addresses this: local JWT signature verification is sub-millisecond on the hot path, an improvement over the current P95 ~18ms internal RPC. For the revocation-sensitive path (introspection), we will document a P95 <50ms requirement for the selected vendor, with a JWKS key-set TTL of 5 minutes to minimize cold-start frequency. The spec will include a load test protocol to validate these targets before cutover.

{"action":"draft_section","result":{},"state":{"doc_type":"decision doc","audience":"engineering leadership","desired_impact":"decision approved by EOQ","template_summary":"(no template specified)","context_dump":"We are proposing to migrate our authentication service from a home-grown implementation to a vendored OIDC (OpenID Connect) provider.

Background / problem: The legacy auth service was built in-house several years ago. It handles session tokens, user identity, and access control. In Q3 2025 it suffered a session-token leak that affected production users. The root cause was a flaw in our token generation and storage logic — exactly the kind of problem that mature, audited OIDC libraries solve by construction. That incident drove the current rebuild conversation.

Current architecture: The in-house service issues opaque session tokens. It owns the full token lifecycle: issuance, validation, revocation. There is no standard protocol surface, so integrations require custom glue. Latency for token validation is an internal RPC, currently measured at P95 ~18ms from the platform layer.

Proposed change: Replace the in-house service with a vendored OIDC provider (e.g. Auth0, Okta, or a self-hosted Keycloak). The provider takes ownership of token issuance and validation. We expose a standard OIDC discovery endpoint. Downstream services validate JWTs locally (no RPC per request), or call the provider's introspection endpoint for revocation-sensitive paths.

Stakeholder map:
- Security team: mostly satisfied with the direction. OIDC is a well-audited standard; moving off a home-grown implementation reduces the attack surface materially. Their remaining question is vendor SOC 2 / penetration-test documentation.
- Platform team: worried about latency. Current P95 token validation is ~18ms (internal RPC). OIDC JWT validation is local and sub-millisecond for the common path, but they are concerned about introspection endpoint latency for revocation-sensitive calls and about cold-start on JWKS key refresh. Need a concrete latency budget in the proposal.
- Product team: wants a timeline. They have features gated on auth improvements (fine-grained scopes, user attribute claims). Blocked until the new service is live. Deadline pressure: they want a spec by end of Q.

Alternatives considered:
- Patch the in-house service: rejected. The Q3 incident revealed deeper structural issues; patching would be whack-a-mole and would not satisfy security.
- Build a new in-house service from scratch to standard: rejected. Engineering cost is high and we would still own all the security surface; the vendored path lets us transfer that risk.

Timeline: spec needed by end of quarter. Engineering leadership needs to approve the decision (this doc) before the spec can be written. The goal is to have that approval by EOQ so the spec work starts next quarter.

Open questions: vendor selection (Auth0 vs Okta vs Keycloak self-hosted), migration path for existing sessions (parallel-run vs hard cutover), latency budget for introspection calls, rollback plan.","sources_consulted":["user-provided task context","stakeholder map from task description","2025-Q3 incident context"],"clarifications":[{"question":"What is the primary driver for the decision: security posture improvement, or engineering cost reduction from maintaining a bespoke service?","self_answer":"Security is the primary driver. **Status: pending approval**

We recommend replacing the in-house authentication service with a vendored OIDC provider. The Q3 2025 session-token incident demonstrated that the structural risk of maintaining bespoke token logic outweighs its benefits. Adopting a standards-based, externally audited provider eliminates that class of risk and unblocks a set of product capabilities that depend on fine-grained identity primitives.

Approval of this doc authorizes writing the technical spec. Vendor selection and migration sequencing are deferred to the spec.","brainstorm_options":["Lead with the ask","Lead with the recommendation","One-sentence recommendation + one-sentence rationale","Include status: pending approval","Name the alternative that was also considered but not chosen"],"kept_options":["Lead with the recommendation","One-sentence recommendation + one-sentence rationale","Include status: pending approval"]},"background":{"drafted_content":"## Background

Our authentication service is a home-grown implementation that has been in production for several years. It issues opaque session tokens and owns the full token lifecycle: issuance, validation, and revocation. There is no standard protocol surface; every service that needs to verify identity calls an internal RPC, currently measuring P95 ~18ms from the platform layer. New integrations require custom glue code written against our internal API.

In Q3 2025, the service suffered a session-token leak affecting production users. The root cause was a flaw in the token generation and storage logic, not a one-off mistake but a structural gap in how the service handles token entropy and storage isolation. A post-incident review concluded that the class of vulnerabilities stems from owning custom cryptographic token logic without the benefit of a continuously audited, battle-tested implementation.

The incident resolved a debate that had been deferred: patching around structural weaknesses is a treadmill, not a solution. The recommendation to migrate to a vendored OIDC provider follows directly from that conclusion.","brainstorm_options":["Describe the current service architecture","Explain opaque token model vs. JWT","Describe the Q3 2025 incident root cause","Quantify impact of the incident","Describe the lack of standard protocol surface","Mention integration burden","Mention current validation latency ~18ms P95"],"kept_options":["Describe the current service architecture","Describe the Q3 2025 incident root cause","Lack of standard protocol surface and integration burden","Current validation latency"]},"proposal":{"drafted_content":"## Proposal

We adopt a vendored OIDC provider as the authoritative identity and token service. The provider issues standard JWTs signed with its private key. Downstream services validate tokens locally by verifying the JWT signature against the provider's public key set (JWKS), cached with a 5-minute TTL. This eliminates the per-request RPC for the common validation path, reducing latency from the current P95 ~18ms to sub-millisecond for the hot path.

For revocation-sensitive flows (explicit logout, forced token invalidation), services call the provider's token introspection endpoint. We will require the chosen provider to meet a P95 introspection latency of <50ms at our request volume, documented in the spec.

The standard OIDC discovery endpoint (`/.well-known/openid-configuration`) removes the need for custom glue in new integrations. It also enables fine-grained OAuth 2.0 scopes and standard user-attribute claims, which unblocks product features currently waiting on identity primitives.

**Candidate providers** (selection deferred to spec phase):
- Auth0 / Okta: managed SaaS, SOC 2 Type II certified, no infrastructure overhead, data residency outside our data centers.
- Keycloak (self-hosted): open source, data stays on-prem, requires our team to operate it.

**Migration approach**: run the new service in parallel while the legacy service drains existing sessions. New logins go to the OIDC provider from day one of the cutover window. This avoids a forced logout of active users and provides a clean rollback path if issues arise during the drain period. The exact cutover schedule is scoped to the spec.","brainstorm_options":["Describe OIDC as a standard","Explain JWT local validation benefit","Explain introspection endpoint for revocation-sensitive paths","Explain JWKS key caching","State latency improvement vs current 18ms RPC","State latency target for introspection endpoint","List candidate vendors and their tradeoffs","Describe migration approach at high level (parallel-run)","Explain standard protocol surface benefits for integrations","Mention fine-grained scopes and claims as product unlock","Describe what the org no longer owns after migration"],"kept_options":["Describe OIDC and JWT local validation benefit","State latency comparison vs current","State introspection endpoint latency budget","List candidate vendors at high level","Describe migration approach at high level","What the org no longer owns"]},"alternatives_rejected":{"drafted_content":"## Alternatives Rejected

**Patch the in-house service.** The Q3 2025 incident review surfaced a class of vulnerabilities, not a single bug. Patching addresses individual symptoms but leaves us owning custom cryptographic logic without the organizational capacity to audit it continuously. Each patch cycle requires rediscovering what the service does and hoping we catch every surface. Security does not consider this path viable.

**Build a new in-house service from scratch to a standard protocol.** This preserves full control over the implementation but shifts the security audit burden to us permanently. We would spend a quarter building what OIDC providers have already validated against adversarial use at scale, and we would still own every future vulnerability in the token logic. The cost is high; the risk transfer is zero. This path was rejected on both efficiency and risk grounds.","brainstorm_options":["Patch the in-house service","Rewrite the in-house service from scratch using standards","Buy a full IAM platform (Okta Workforce, etc.)","Do nothing","Explain why patching fails","Explain why rewriting in-house fails","Note that 'do nothing' is off the table after Q3 incident"],"kept_options":["Patch the in-house service with explanation","Rewrite in-house from scratch with explanation"]},"stakeholder_positions":{"drafted_content":"## Stakeholder Positions

**Security** -- Supportive. The shift to a well-audited standard implementation materially reduces the attack surface. Their remaining requirement: the chosen vendor must provide current SOC 2 Type II certification and a recent third-party penetration test report before the spec is finalized. This is a due-diligence gate in the spec phase, not a blocker on this decision.

**Platform** -- Cautious. The concern is latency regression on token validation paths. The proposal directly addresses this: local JWT signature verification is sub-millisecond on the hot path, an improvement over the current P95 ~18ms internal RPC. For the revocation-sensitive path (introspection), we will document a P95 <50ms requirement for the selected vendor, with a JWKS key-set TTL of 5 minutes to minimize cold-start frequency. The spec will include a load test protocol to validate these targets before cutover.

**Product** -- Wants a timeline and wants to know this unblocks their roadmap. Approval of this doc starts the spec in Q1 next year. The spec will define the delivery timeline. Once the OIDC provider is live, fine-grained OAuth 2.0 scopes and standard user-attribute claims are available by design, without additional auth engineering work. That unblocks the identity-dependent features currently in the product backlog.","brainstorm_options":["Security team: satisfied, open item is SOC2 docs","Platform team: worried about latency, specifically introspection and JWKS refresh","Product team: wants timeline, wants scopes and claims","Frame each stakeholder as: position / concern / how proposal addresses it","Use a table vs. prose","Mention security team's remaining ask (vendor pentest docs)","Address platform concern with concrete numbers","Address product concern with what this unblocks"],"kept_options":["Frame each as position + concern + response","Security: SOC2 remaining ask","Platform: latency with concrete numbers response","Product: timeline and what it unblocks"]},"open_questions":{"drafted_content":"## Open Questions

The following are intentionally deferred to the spec phase. None of them block this decision.

1. **Vendor selection.** Auth0, Okta, and self-hosted Keycloak are the current candidates. The spec phase will run a structured evaluation covering SOC 2 status, data residency, latency benchmarks, pricing, and operational overhead.

2. **Compliance and data residency.** A quick review of our regulatory obligations (GDPR, SOC 2 scope, any sector-specific requirements) is needed to determine whether user identity data can reside on a third-party SaaS platform or must stay on-prem. This is a gate on vendor selection, not on the decision to adopt OIDC.

3. **Migration sequencing.** Parallel-run is the preferred approach, but the drain-window duration, session expiry strategy, and cutover rollback criteria need to be specified and agreed with platform.

4. **Rollback plan.** The spec will define the conditions under which we revert to the legacy service during the parallel-run window and how state is preserved.","brainstorm_options":["Vendor selection: Auth0 vs Okta vs Keycloak","Compliance requirements constraining SaaS vs self-hosted","Migration strategy detail: parallel-run sequencing","Session drain window duration","Rollback plan","Introspection endpoint load testing methodology","JWKS TTL configuration per service"],"kept_options":["Vendor selection","Compliance/data residency constraint check","Migration sequencing and drain window","Rollback plan","Load test methodology"]},"ask_and_timeline":null},"final_review_notes":"","reader_questions":[],"reader_results":[],"reader_issues":[],"has_blocking_issues":false,"final_doc":null,"doc_summary":null,"current_prompt":"STAGE 2 of 3: REFINEMENT AND STRUCTURE (drafting).

The following are intentionally deferred to the spec phase. None of them block this decision.

{"action":"complete_drafting","result":{},"state":{"doc_type":"decision doc","audience":"engineering leadership","desired_impact":"decision approved by EOQ","template_summary":"(no template specified)","context_dump":"We are proposing to migrate our authentication service from a home-grown implementation to a vendored OIDC (OpenID Connect) provider.

Background / problem: The legacy auth service was built in-house several years ago. It handles session tokens, user identity, and access control. In Q3 2025 it suffered a session-token leak that affected production users. The root cause was a flaw in our token generation and storage logic — exactly the kind of problem that mature, audited OIDC libraries solve by construction. That incident drove the current rebuild conversation.

Current architecture: The in-house service issues opaque session tokens. It owns the full token lifecycle: issuance, validation, revocation. There is no standard protocol surface, so integrations require custom glue. Latency for token validation is an internal RPC, currently measured at P95 ~18ms from the platform layer.

Proposed change: Replace the in-house service with a vendored OIDC provider (e.g. Auth0, Okta, or a self-hosted Keycloak). The provider takes ownership of token issuance and validation. We expose a standard OIDC discovery endpoint. Downstream services validate JWTs locally (no RPC per request), or call the provider's introspection endpoint for revocation-sensitive paths.

Stakeholder map:
- Security team: mostly satisfied with the direction. OIDC is a well-audited standard; moving off a home-grown implementation reduces the attack surface materially. Their remaining question is vendor SOC 2 / penetration-test documentation.
- Platform team: worried about latency. Current P95 token validation is ~18ms (internal RPC). OIDC JWT validation is local and sub-millisecond for the common path, but they are concerned about introspection endpoint latency for revocation-sensitive calls and about cold-start on JWKS key refresh. Need a concrete latency budget in the proposal.
- Product team: wants a timeline. They have features gated on auth improvements (fine-grained scopes, user attribute claims). Blocked until the new service is live. Deadline pressure: they want a spec by end of Q.

Alternatives considered:
- Patch the in-house service: rejected. The Q3 incident revealed deeper structural issues; patching would be whack-a-mole and would not satisfy security.
- Build a new in-house service from scratch to standard: rejected. Engineering cost is high and we would still own all the security surface; the vendored path lets us transfer that risk.

Timeline: spec needed by end of quarter. Engineering leadership needs to approve the decision (this doc) before the spec can be written. The goal is to have that approval by EOQ so the spec work starts next quarter.

Open questions: vendor selection (Auth0 vs Okta vs Keycloak self-hosted), migration path for existing sessions (parallel-run vs hard cutover), latency budget for introspection calls, rollback plan.","sources_consulted":["user-provided task context","stakeholder map from task description","2025-Q3 incident context"],"clarifications":[{"question":"What is the primary driver for the decision: security posture improvement, or engineering cost reduction from maintaining a bespoke service?","self_answer":"Security is the primary driver. **Status: pending approval**

We recommend replacing the in-house authentication service with a vendored OIDC provider. The Q3 2025 session-token incident demonstrated that the structural risk of maintaining bespoke token logic outweighs its benefits. Adopting a standards-based, externally audited provider eliminates that class of risk and unblocks a set of product capabilities that depend on fine-grained identity primitives.

Approval of this doc authorizes writing the technical spec. Vendor selection and migration sequencing are deferred to the spec.","brainstorm_options":["Lead with the ask","Lead with the recommendation","One-sentence recommendation + one-sentence rationale","Include status: pending approval","Name the alternative that was also considered but not chosen"],"kept_options":["Lead with the recommendation","One-sentence recommendation + one-sentence rationale","Include status: pending approval"]},"background":{"drafted_content":"## Background

Our authentication service is a home-grown implementation that has been in production for several years. It issues opaque session tokens and owns the full token lifecycle: issuance, validation, and revocation. There is no standard protocol surface; every service that needs to verify identity calls an internal RPC, currently measuring P95 ~18ms from the platform layer. New integrations require custom glue code written against our internal API.

In Q3 2025, the service suffered a session-token leak affecting production users. The root cause was a flaw in the token generation and storage logic, not a one-off mistake but a structural gap in how the service handles token entropy and storage isolation. A post-incident review concluded that the class of vulnerabilities stems from owning custom cryptographic token logic without the benefit of a continuously audited, battle-tested implementation.

The incident resolved a debate that had been deferred: patching around structural weaknesses is a treadmill, not a solution. The recommendation to migrate to a vendored OIDC provider follows directly from that conclusion.","brainstorm_options":["Describe the current service architecture","Explain opaque token model vs. JWT","Describe the Q3 2025 incident root cause","Quantify impact of the incident","Describe the lack of standard protocol surface","Mention integration burden","Mention current validation latency ~18ms P95"],"kept_options":["Describe the current service architecture","Describe the Q3 2025 incident root cause","Lack of standard protocol surface and integration burden","Current validation latency"]},"proposal":{"drafted_content":"## Proposal

We adopt a vendored OIDC provider as the authoritative identity and token service. The provider issues standard JWTs signed with its private key. Downstream services validate tokens locally by verifying the JWT signature against the provider's public key set (JWKS), cached with a 5-minute TTL. This eliminates the per-request RPC for the common validation path, reducing latency from the current P95 ~18ms to sub-millisecond for the hot path.

For revocation-sensitive flows (explicit logout, forced token invalidation), services call the provider's token introspection endpoint. We will require the chosen provider to meet a P95 introspection latency of <50ms at our request volume, documented in the spec.

The standard OIDC discovery endpoint (`/.well-known/openid-configuration`) removes the need for custom glue in new integrations. It also enables fine-grained OAuth 2.0 scopes and standard user-attribute claims, which unblocks product features currently waiting on identity primitives.

**Candidate providers** (selection deferred to spec phase):
- Auth0 / Okta: managed SaaS, SOC 2 Type II certified, no infrastructure overhead, data residency outside our data centers.
- Keycloak (self-hosted): open source, data stays on-prem, requires our team to operate it.

**Migration approach**: run the new service in parallel while the legacy service drains existing sessions. New logins go to the OIDC provider from day one of the cutover window. This avoids a forced logout of active users and provides a clean rollback path if issues arise during the drain period.
