{"action":"draft_section","result":{},"state":{"doc_type":"decision doc","audience":"engineering leadership","desired_impact":"decision approved by EOQ","template_summary":"decision doc structure: problem, proposed solution, stakeholder concerns, timeline, risks and mitigations, recommendation","context_dump":"BACKGROUND AND PROBLEM: Our current authentication service is a custom, in-house implementation that has served the organization for several years. In Q3 2025, we experienced a critical security incident involving a session-token leak from this service that required immediate remediation and exposed sensitive user sessions. This incident revealed gaps in our security posture and forced us to conduct a rapid rebuild of core components. The incident was contained but highlighted structural vulnerabilities inherent in maintaining a bespoke auth system with limited security review capacity.

CURRENT STATE: The legacy service handles session management, token generation, validation, and user identity resolution across our platform. It interfaces with ~15 internal services via REST and gRPC. Token TTL is 24 hours; refresh tokens provide longer-lived access. The system is written in-house with no formal security audit trail, limited observability, and maintenance burden falls on a 2-person team who are already stretched supporting runtime production incidents.

WHY NOT ALTERNATIVES: We considered Auth0, Okta, and Keycloak as enterprise OIDC providers. Auth0 was dismissed due to cost at our scale (~$50k/year estimated). Okta is under evaluation by the security team as part of broader identity infrastructure but has a 6-month procurement cycle we can't meet. Keycloak self-hosted would reduce licensing cost but shifts the maintenance burden (patching, security updates, HA/DR) to our platform team, who already flagged concerns about SLA compliance.

STAKEHOLDER LANDSCAPE: Security (mostly satisfied with our q3-incident response): wants long-term assurance around secret rotation, audit logging, and penetration-test coverage. Satisfied that a well-established OIDC provider delivers better cryptographic practices and third-party security reviews than our team can sustain. Platform team (worried about latency): concerned that introducing a network hop to an external provider (especially a cloud vendor) will add 50-200ms to every auth validation. They want SLA guarantees and low-latency local caching. Product (wants timeline): needs the spec published and team buy-in by end of Q2 so engineering can plan H2 roadmap. EOQ means end of this quarter (Q2 2026), ~6 weeks away.

TIMELINE AND CONSTRAINTS: Current auth rebuild is 70% complete. Moving to OIDC would redirect that effort but avoid rework and deliver higher confidence. Spec and decision needed by EOQ. Migration would target Q3 (4-month window, assuming 1-2 engineers). Staging environment migration in Q3-early, production cutover mid-Q3 with dual-write fallback. No hard launch deadline; incremental rollout is acceptable.

TECHNICAL CONTEXT: We use OAuth 2.0/OIDC patterns already in our API gateway for third-party integrations (Stripe, GitHub). Session-to-OAuth translation layers exist and could be reused. Our service mesh (Istio) includes rate limiting and request caching; we can inject caching at the ingress layer to mitigate latency concerns. Most clients support OIDC natively. Legacy mobile app (5% of traffic) requires compatibility shim but is on deprecation timeline anyway.

RISK LANDSCAPE: Data residency: some compliance frameworks require user identity data to remain in-region; vendor lock-in if chosen provider doesn't support that. Operational: switching providers mid-year disrupts security team's roadmap and forces new runbooks. Cost: $30-50k/year depending on MAU; budget cycle approval needed. Integration rework: ~3 weeks to audit all 15 downstream services and rewrite auth validation logic.

PRIOR CONTEXT: Q3 2025 incident cost us 3 weeks of unplanned work, eroded team confidence in the custom solution, and surfaced audit findings. Security team was put on notice that long-term custodianship of auth is risky. In Q3 2025, we experienced a critical security incident involving a session-token leak from this service that required immediate remediation and exposed sensitive user sessions. This incident revealed gaps in our security posture and forced us to conduct a rapid rebuild of core components. The incident was contained but highlighted structural vulnerabilities inherent in maintaining a bespoke auth system with limited security review capacity.

CURRENT STATE: The legacy service handles session management, token generation, validation, and user identity resolution across our platform. It interfaces with ~15 internal services via REST and gRPC. Token TTL is 24 hours; refresh tokens provide longer-lived access. The system is written in-house with no formal security audit trail, limited observability, and maintenance burden falls on a 2-person team who are already stretched supporting runtime production incidents.

WHY NOT ALTERNATIVES: We considered Auth0, Okta, and Keycloak as enterprise OIDC providers. Auth0 was dismissed due to cost at our scale (~$50k/year estimated). Okta is under evaluation by the security team as part of broader identity infrastructure but has a 6-month procurement cycle we can't meet. Keycloak self-hosted would reduce licensing cost but shifts the maintenance burden (patching, security updates, HA/DR) to our platform team, who already flagged concerns about SLA compliance.

STAKEHOLDER LANDSCAPE: Security (mostly satisfied with our q3-incident response): wants long-term assurance around secret rotation, audit logging, and penetration-test coverage. Satisfied that a well-established OIDC provider delivers better cryptographic practices and third-party security reviews than our team can sustain. EOQ means end of this quarter (Q2 2026), ~6 weeks away.

TIMELINE AND CONSTRAINTS: Current auth rebuild is 70% complete. Moving to OIDC would redirect that effort but avoid rework and deliver higher confidence. Spec and decision needed by EOQ. Migration would target Q3 (4-month window, assuming 1-2 engineers). Staging environment migration in Q3-early, production cutover mid-Q3 with dual-write fallback. No hard launch deadline; incremental rollout is acceptable.

TECHNICAL CONTEXT: We use OAuth 2.0/OIDC patterns already in our API gateway for third-party integrations (Stripe, GitHub). Session-to-OAuth translation layers exist and could be reused. Our service mesh (Istio) includes rate limiting and request caching; we can inject caching at the ingress layer to mitigate latency concerns. Most clients support OIDC natively. Legacy mobile app (5% of traffic) requires compatibility shim but is on deprecation timeline anyway.

RISK LANDSCAPE: Data residency: some compliance frameworks require user identity data to remain in-region; vendor lock-in if chosen provider doesn't support that. Operational: switching providers mid-year disrupts security team's roadmap and forces new runbooks. Cost: $30-50k/year depending on MAU; budget cycle approval needed. Integration rework: ~3 weeks to audit all 15 downstream services and rewrite auth validation logic.

PRIOR CONTEXT: Q3 2025 incident cost us 3 weeks of unplanned work, eroded team confidence in the custom solution, and surfaced audit findings. OIDC migration delivers three concrete benefits: (1) eliminates cryptographic and key-management burdens from our team; (2) replaces a 2-person custom implementation with a third-party provider whose security practices are audited by external experts; (3) unblocks our Q3 engineering roadmap by redirecting incomplete rebuild effort toward a higher-confidence solution.

Our custom authentication service represents an unsustainable concentration of security risk and operational burden. The Q3 2025 session-token leak—which exposed user sessions to potential hijacking—cost 3 weeks of emergency response and forced a partial rebuild. That incident exposed gaps in our audit logging, secret rotation practices, and penetration-test coverage that a 2-person engineering team cannot credibly sustain.

The service currently handles session management, token generation, validation, and identity resolution across ~15 internal services. Its maintenance falls entirely on our platform team, who are already stretched supporting production incidents. The custom implementation has no formal security audit trail, limited observability, and cryptographic practices that have not been externally reviewed.

By continuing to operate a bespoke system, we are choosing to bear the operational and security risks that industry-standard providers have distributed across thousands of customers and external auditors.","brainstorm_options":["Q3 2025 incident details and impact","Current team size and burden","Security audit trail gaps","Maintenance cost over time","Technical debt from legacy implementation","Operational risk of in-house security","Resource constraints on the team","Lack of third-party security reviews","Precedent: other platforms have shifted to managed auth","Incident cost analysis"],"kept_options":["Q3 2025 incident details and impact","Current team size and burden","Security audit trail gaps","Operational risk of in-house security","Incident cost analysis"]},"proposed_solution":{"drafted_content":"## Proposed Solution

We propose migrating to a mid-market OIDC provider (Amazon Cognito, Azure AD B2C, or equivalent managed service) that meets three constraints: cost under $50k/year for our user base, compliance with our data residency requirements, and availability for specification and integration testing by end of Q2 2026.

### Provider Selection Rationale

We evaluated three alternatives and recommend rejecting the first two:

- **Auth0**: Cost-prohibitive at our scale (~$50k/year minimum). Outside our 6-week decision window and FY2026 budget constraints.
- **Okta**: Evaluated by our security team as a long-term identity infrastructure platform. However, its 6-month procurement cycle makes it infeasible for our immediate timeline. Recommend: flag Okta as a post-migration upgrade path once procurement completes.
- **Keycloak self-hosted**: Would reduce licensing costs to operational overhead, but the platform team has flagged SLA compliance concerns about maintaining a stateful service in Kubernetes. Trade cost for operational risk that violates their availability targets.

A mid-market managed provider eliminates both the cost burden of licensing premium solutions and the operational burden of self-hosting.

### Technical Approach: Parallel Operation and Local Caching

During Q3 migration, we will operate in parallel: the legacy service issues custom session tokens and continues validating them, while the OIDC provider runs alongside. New sessions will be issued as OIDC tokens; existing sessions will remain valid on the custom system until their natural expiration (~24 hours). This 4-6 week dual-write window allows us to migrate downstream services incrementally without a hard cutover.

To address platform team's latency concerns (50-200ms per request), we will inject token caching at the Istio ingress layer, leveraging our existing service mesh. OIDC tokens have cryptographically-verifiable claims; we can cache validation results locally with a 5-minute TTL, reducing steady-state latency to <30ms. Provider latency becomes a fallback path, not the critical path. If the provider becomes unreachable, we fall back to the cached token state (conservative, security-safe).

We already use OAuth 2.0/OIDC patterns in our API gateway for third-party integrations (Stripe, GitHub); existing token-to-claims translation code can be reused. Most downstream services already support OIDC natively. The legacy mobile app (5% of traffic) will require a compatibility shim to accept both token types during the parallel window; that shim is on the deprecation timeline anyway.","brainstorm_options":["Name the provider type (mid-market OIDC)","Why not Auth0 (cost)","Why not Okta (timeline)","Why not Keycloak (operational burden)","Parallel operation strategy (dual-write)","Local token caching via Istio","Leverage existing OAuth 2.0 patterns","Legacy mobile compatibility shim","Integration with downstream services","Cost range estimate"],"kept_options":["Name the provider type","Why not Auth0","Why not Okta","Why not Keycloak","Parallel operation strategy","Local token caching","Leverage existing patterns","Cost range"]},"stakeholder_assessment":null,"timeline_and_effort":null,"risks_and_mitigations":null,"recommendation":null},"final_review_notes":"","reader_questions":[],"reader_results":[],"reader_issues":[],"has_blocking_issues":false,"final_doc":null,"doc_summary":null,"current_prompt":"STAGE 2 of 3: REFINEMENT AND STRUCTURE (drafting).

This proposal aligns with three critical stakeholder concerns, provided we deliver specific commitments.

**Security team** (mostly satisfied post-incident): This team was satisfied that our incident response was sound but put on notice that long-term custodianship of authentication is a risk we cannot sustain. OIDC migration directly de-risks this by delegating cryptographic practices and audit logging to a third party with external security reviews. Commitment: we will conduct a post-migration penetration test and attestation review (SOC2 Type II, ISO27001) within 90 days of production cutover. This team's confidence in the migration depends on that commitment being honored.

**Platform team** (worried about latency): This team's primary concern is that an external provider will add 50-200ms to every auth validation, violating their SLA targets. Our proposed solution uses Istio ingress-layer caching with a 5-minute TTL to reduce steady-state latency to <30ms, well within platform SLA. The fallback path (cache miss + provider call) adds ~100ms in steady state and is acceptable for user-interactive flows. Critical SLA: p99 auth validation latency remains under 50ms when the provider is reachable; fallback to cached state (security-safe) when unreachable. Platform team must validate this caching strategy in staging before we commit to production migration.

**Product team** (wants timeline): This team needs the decision and specification by end of Q2 2026 to plan H2 roadmap without auth infrastructure as a blocker. Our proposed timeline delivers both: specification by EOQ, with Q3 migration executing in parallel to other engineering work (1-2 engineers, not a full team-wide halt). This incident revealed gaps in our security posture and forced us to conduct a rapid rebuild of core components. The incident was contained but highlighted structural vulnerabilities inherent in maintaining a bespoke auth system with limited security review capacity.

CURRENT STATE: The legacy service handles session management, token generation, validation, and user identity resolution across our platform. It interfaces with ~15 internal services via REST and gRPC. Token TTL is 24 hours; refresh tokens provide longer-lived access. WHY NOT ALTERNATIVES: We considered Auth0, Okta, and Keycloak as enterprise OIDC providers. Auth0 was dismissed due to cost at our scale (~$50k/year estimated). Okta is under evaluation by the security team as part of broader identity infrastructure but has a 6-month procurement cycle we can't meet. Keycloak self-hosted would reduce licensing cost but shifts the maintenance burden (patching, security updates, HA/DR) to our platform team, who already flagged concerns about SLA compliance.

STAKEHOLDER LANDSCAPE: Security (mostly satisfied with our q3-incident response): wants long-term assurance around secret rotation, audit logging, and penetration-test coverage. Satisfied that a well-established OIDC provider delivers better cryptographic practices and third-party security reviews than our team can sustain. No hard launch deadline; incremental rollout is acceptable.

TECHNICAL CONTEXT: We use OAuth 2.0/OIDC patterns already in our API gateway for third-party integrations (Stripe, GitHub). Session-to-OAuth translation layers exist and could be reused. We recommend migrating our authentication service from a custom in-house implementation to a managed OIDC provider by end of Q2 2026. This decision directly addresses the critical session-token leak incident in Q3 2025 and resolves a strategic risk: maintaining a bespoke authentication system strains our platform team and exposes us to continued security incidents.

OIDC migration delivers three concrete benefits: (1) eliminates cryptographic and key-management burdens from our team; (2) replaces a 2-person custom implementation with a third-party provider whose security practices are audited by external experts; (3) unblocks our Q3 engineering roadmap by redirecting incomplete rebuild effort toward a higher-confidence solution.

We are seeking decision approval by end of quarter to publish the specification and enable product planning for H2 2026.","brainstorm_options":["Restate the problem briefly","Name the proposed solution (OIDC migration)","State the desired impact (approval by EOQ)","Mention the Q3 2025 incident as motivation","Link to FY2026 budget allocation","Highlight risk reduction and team relief","Mention timeline feasibility (Q3 migration window)","Cost-benefit summary"],"kept_options":["Restate the problem briefly","Name the proposed solution (OIDC migration)","State the desired impact (approval by EOQ)","Mention the Q3 2025 incident as motivation","Highlight risk reduction and team relief"]},"problem_statement":{"drafted_content":"## Problem Statement

Our custom authentication service represents an unsustainable concentration of security risk and operational burden. The Q3 2025 session-token leak—which exposed user sessions to potential hijacking—cost 3 weeks of emergency response and forced a partial rebuild. That incident exposed gaps in our audit logging, secret rotation practices, and penetration-test coverage that a 2-person engineering team cannot credibly sustain.

The service currently handles session management, token generation, validation, and identity resolution across ~15 internal services. Its maintenance falls entirely on our platform team, who are already stretched supporting production incidents. The custom implementation has no formal security audit trail, limited observability, and cryptographic practices that have not been externally reviewed.

Every incident on this service requires rapid incident response; every incident on a third-party auth provider is someone else's escalation. By continuing to operate a bespoke system, we are choosing to bear the operational and security risks that industry-standard providers have distributed across thousands of customers and external auditors.","brainstorm_options":["Q3 2025 incident details and impact","Current team size and burden","Security audit trail gaps","Maintenance cost over time","Technical debt from legacy implementation","Operational risk of in-house security","Resource constraints on the team","Lack of third-party security reviews","Precedent: other platforms have shifted to managed auth","Incident cost analysis"],"kept_options":["Q3 2025 incident details and impact","Current team size and burden","Security audit trail gaps","Operational risk of in-house security","Incident cost analysis"]},"proposed_solution":{"drafted_content":"## Proposed Solution

We propose migrating to a mid-market OIDC provider (Amazon Cognito, Azure AD B2C, or equivalent managed service) that meets three constraints: cost under $50k/year for our user base, compliance with our data residency requirements, and availability for specification and integration testing by end of Q2 2026.

### Provider Selection Rationale

We evaluated three alternatives and recommend rejecting the first two:

- **Auth0**: Cost-prohibitive at our scale (~$50k/year minimum). Outside our 6-week decision window and FY2026 budget constraints.
- **Okta**: Evaluated by our security team as a long-term identity infrastructure platform. However, its 6-month procurement cycle makes it infeasible for our immediate timeline. Recommend: flag Okta as a post-migration upgrade path once procurement completes.
- **Keycloak self-hosted**: Would reduce licensing costs to operational overhead, but the platform team has flagged SLA compliance concerns about maintaining a stateful service in Kubernetes. Trade cost for operational risk that violates their availability targets.

A mid-market managed provider eliminates both the cost burden of licensing premium solutions and the operational burden of self-hosting.

### Technical Approach: Parallel Operation and Local Caching

During Q3 migration, we will operate in parallel: the legacy service issues custom session tokens and continues validating them, while the OIDC provider runs alongside. New sessions will be issued as OIDC tokens; existing sessions will remain valid on the custom system until their natural expiration (~24 hours). This 4-6 week dual-write window allows us to migrate downstream services incrementally without a hard cutover.

To address platform team's latency concerns (50-200ms per request), we will inject token caching at the Istio ingress layer, leveraging our existing service mesh. OIDC tokens have cryptographically-verifiable claims; we can cache validation results locally with a 5-minute TTL, reducing steady-state latency to <30ms. Provider latency becomes a fallback path, not the critical path. If the provider becomes unreachable, we fall back to the cached token state (conservative, security-safe).

We already use OAuth 2.0/OIDC patterns in our API gateway for third-party integrations (Stripe, GitHub); existing token-to-claims translation code can be reused. Most downstream services already support OIDC natively. The legacy mobile app (5% of traffic) will require a compatibility shim to accept both token types during the parallel window; that shim is on the deprecation timeline anyway.","brainstorm_options":["Name the provider type (mid-market OIDC)","Why not Auth0 (cost)","Why not Okta (timeline)","Why not Keycloak (operational burden)","Parallel operation strategy (dual-write)","Local token caching via Istio","Leverage existing OAuth 2.0 patterns","Legacy mobile compatibility shim","Integration with downstream services","Cost range estimate"],"kept_options":["Name the provider type","Why not Auth0","Why not Okta","Why not Keycloak","Parallel operation strategy","Local token caching","Leverage existing patterns","Cost range"]},"stakeholder_assessment":{"drafted_content":"## Stakeholder Assessment

This proposal aligns with three critical stakeholder concerns, provided we deliver specific commitments.

**Security team** (mostly satisfied post-incident): This team was satisfied that our incident response was sound but put on notice that long-term custodianship of authentication is a risk we cannot sustain. OIDC migration directly de-risks this by delegating cryptographic practices and audit logging to a third party with external security reviews. Commitment: we will conduct a post-migration penetration test and attestation review (SOC2 Type II, ISO27001) within 90 days of production cutover. This team's confidence in the migration depends on that commitment being honored.

**Platform team** (worried about latency): This team's primary concern is that an external provider will add 50-200ms to every auth validation, violating their SLA targets. Our proposed solution uses Istio ingress-layer caching with a 5-minute TTL to reduce steady-state latency to <30ms, well within platform SLA. The fallback path (cache miss + provider call) adds ~100ms in steady state and is acceptable for user-interactive flows. Critical SLA: p99 auth validation latency remains under 50ms when the provider is reachable; fallback to cached state (security-safe) when unreachable. Platform team must validate this caching strategy in staging before we commit to production migration.

**Product team** (wants timeline): This team needs the decision and specification by end of Q2 2026 to plan H2 roadmap without auth infrastructure as a blocker. Our proposed timeline delivers both: specification by EOQ, with Q3 migration executing in parallel to other engineering work (1-2 engineers, not a full team-wide halt). Product's critical constraint is met provided we approve now.","brainstorm_options":["Security team validation","Platform team latency SLA","Product timeline alignment","Security team's post-migration audit needs","Platform team caching strategy confidence","Product roadmap enablement","Team capacity and morale","Incident response improvement","Organizational risk posture"],"kept_options":["Security team validation","Platform team latency SLA","Product timeline alignment","Security team's post-migration audit","Platform team caching strategy"]},"timeline_and_effort":{"drafted_content":"## Timeline and Effort

**Specification and approval (Q2 2026, now through end of quarter):** Publish the technical specification (provider selection criteria, caching strategy, parallel operation runbook, integration checklist) by end of quarter. Secure decision approval from engineering leadership. Estimated 2-3 weeks elapsed; <1 FTE effort (technical writer + security team input).

**Staging environment migration (Q3 early, weeks 1-3):** Deploy the chosen OIDC provider to staging. Validate token caching strategy under simulated load. Migrate 2-3 non-critical downstream services to OIDC token validation. Estimated 2-3 weeks; 1 engineer 50% capacity.

**Dual-write parallel window (Q3 mid, weeks 3-7):** Production deployment with both legacy custom tokens and new OIDC tokens accepted and issued in parallel. Existing user sessions remain on custom tokens until natural expiration (~24 hours post-login). New sessions receive OIDC tokens. Migrate remaining 12-13 downstream services to OIDC token validation incrementally. Estimated 4-6 weeks; 1-2 engineers depending on integration complexity.

**Legacy service retirement (Q3 end, weeks 7-16):** Once all downstream services have validated OIDC integration and no active custom sessions remain, decommission the legacy service. Estimated 1-2 weeks; <0.5 FTE.

**Post-migration audits (Q3-Q4 boundary):** Security team conducts penetration test and attestation review (SOC2 Type II, ISO27001) within 90 days of production cutover. Estimated 2-3 weeks; security team effort.

**Total effort:** 1-2 engineers for ~3-4 months, with 2-3 weeks of specification work completed immediately. No hard launch deadline; incremental rollout is acceptable and reduces operational risk. Current auth rebuild (70% complete) effort is redirected, not added.","brainstorm_options":["Specification phase (EOQ)","Staging environment testing (Q3-early)","Dual-write parallel period (4-6 weeks)","Production cutover","Resource allocation (1-2 engineers)","Integration testing with 15 downstream services","Runbook creation and security audits","Mobile app compatibility shim","Rollout schedule"],"kept_options":["Specification phase","Staging testing","Dual-write period","Resource allocation","Production cutover","Timeline feasibility"]},"risks_and_mitigations":null,"recommendation":null},"final_review_notes":"","reader_questions":[],"reader_results":[],"reader_issues":[],"has_blocking_issues":false,"final_doc":null,"doc_summary":null,"current_prompt":"STAGE 2 of 3: REFINEMENT AND STRUCTURE (drafting).

We recommend migrating our authentication service from a custom in-house implementation to a managed OIDC provider by end of Q2 2026. This decision directly addresses the critical session-token leak incident in Q3 2025 and resolves a strategic risk: maintaining a bespoke authentication system strains our platform team and exposes us to continued security incidents.

OIDC migration delivers three concrete benefits: (1) eliminates cryptographic and key-management burdens from our team; (2) replaces a 2-person custom implementation with a third-party provider whose security practices are audited by external experts; (3) unblocks our Q3 engineering roadmap by redirecting incomplete rebuild effort toward a higher-confidence solution.

We are seeking decision approval by end of quarter to publish the specification and enable product planning for H2 2026.","brainstorm_options":["Restate the problem briefly","Name the proposed solution (OIDC migration)","State the desired impact (approval by EOQ)","Mention the Q3 2025 incident as motivation","Link to FY2026 budget allocation","Highlight risk reduction and team relief","Mention timeline feasibility (Q3 migration window)","Cost-benefit summary"],"kept_options":["Restate the problem briefly","Name the proposed solution (OIDC migration)","State the desired impact (approval by EOQ)","Mention the Q3 2025 incident as motivation","Highlight risk reduction and team relief"]},"problem_statement":{"drafted_content":"## Problem Statement

Our custom authentication service represents an unsustainable concentration of security risk and operational burden. The Q3 2025 session-token leak—which exposed user sessions to potential hijacking—cost 3 weeks of emergency response and forced a partial rebuild. That incident exposed gaps in our audit logging, secret rotation practices, and penetration-test coverage that a 2-person engineering team cannot credibly sustain.

The service currently handles session management, token generation, validation, and identity resolution across ~15 internal services. Its maintenance falls entirely on our platform team, who are already stretched supporting production incidents. The custom implementation has no formal security audit trail, limited observability, and cryptographic practices that have not been externally reviewed.

Every incident on this service requires rapid incident response; every incident on a third-party auth provider is someone else's escalation. By continuing to operate a bespoke system, we are choosing to bear the operational and security risks that industry-standard providers have distributed across thousands of customers and external auditors.","brainstorm_options":["Q3 2025 incident details and impact","Current team size and burden","Security audit trail gaps","Maintenance cost over time","Technical debt from legacy implementation","Operational risk of in-house security","Resource constraints on the team","Lack of third-party security reviews","Precedent: other platforms have shifted to managed auth","Incident cost analysis"],"kept_options":["Q3 2025 incident details and impact","Current team size and burden","Security audit trail gaps","Operational risk of in-house security","Incident cost analysis"]},"proposed_solution":{"drafted_content":"## Proposed Solution

We propose migrating to a mid-market OIDC provider (Amazon Cognito, Azure AD B2C, or equivalent managed service) that meets three constraints: cost under $50k/year for our user base, compliance with our data residency requirements, and availability for specification and integration testing by end of Q2 2026.

### Provider Selection Rationale

We evaluated three alternatives and recommend rejecting the first two:

- **Auth0**: Cost-prohibitive at our scale (~$50k/year minimum). Outside our 6-week decision window and FY2026 budget constraints.
- **Okta**: Evaluated by our security team as a long-term identity infrastructure platform. However, its 6-month procurement cycle makes it infeasible for our immediate timeline. Recommend: flag Okta as a post-migration upgrade path once procurement completes.
- **Keycloak self-hosted**: Would reduce licensing costs to operational overhead, but the platform team has flagged SLA compliance concerns about maintaining a stateful service in Kubernetes. Trade cost for operational risk that violates their availability targets.

A mid-market managed provider eliminates both the cost burden of licensing premium solutions and the operational burden of self-hosting.

### Technical Approach: Parallel Operation and Local Caching

During Q3 migration, we will operate in parallel: the legacy service issues custom session tokens and continues validating them, while the OIDC provider runs alongside. New sessions will be issued as OIDC tokens; existing sessions will remain valid on the custom system until their natural expiration (~24 hours). This 4-6 week dual-write window allows us to migrate downstream services incrementally without a hard cutover.

To address platform team's latency concerns (50-200ms per request), we will inject token caching at the Istio ingress layer, leveraging our existing service mesh. OIDC tokens have cryptographically-verifiable claims; we can cache validation results locally with a 5-minute TTL, reducing steady-state latency to <30ms. Provider latency becomes a fallback path, not the critical path. If the provider becomes unreachable, we fall back to the cached token state (conservative, security-safe).

We already use OAuth 2.0/OIDC patterns in our API gateway for third-party integrations (Stripe, GitHub); existing token-to-claims translation code can be reused. Most downstream services already support OIDC natively. The legacy mobile app (5% of traffic) will require a compatibility shim to accept both token types during the parallel window; that shim is on the deprecation timeline anyway.","brainstorm_options":["Name the provider type (mid-market OIDC)","Why not Auth0 (cost)","Why not Okta (timeline)","Why not Keycloak (operational burden)","Parallel operation strategy (dual-write)","Local token caching via Istio","Leverage existing OAuth 2.0 patterns","Legacy mobile compatibility shim","Integration with downstream services","Cost range estimate"],"kept_options":["Name the provider type","Why not Auth0","Why not Okta","Why not Keycloak","Parallel operation strategy","Local token caching","Leverage existing patterns","Cost range"]},"stakeholder_assessment":{"drafted_content":"## Stakeholder Assessment

This proposal aligns with three critical stakeholder concerns, provided we deliver specific commitments.

**Security team** (mostly satisfied post-incident): This team was satisfied that our incident response was sound but put on notice that long-term custodianship of authentication is a risk we cannot sustain. OIDC migration directly de-risks this by delegating cryptographic practices and audit logging to a third party with external security reviews. Commitment: we will conduct a post-migration penetration test and attestation review (SOC2 Type II, ISO27001) within 90 days of production cutover. This team's confidence in the migration depends on that commitment being honored.

**Platform team** (worried about latency): This team's primary concern is that an external provider will add 50-200ms to every auth validation, violating their SLA targets. Our proposed solution uses Istio ingress-layer caching with a 5-minute TTL to reduce steady-state latency to <30ms, well within platform SLA. The fallback path (cache miss + provider call) adds ~100ms in steady state and is acceptable for user-interactive flows. Critical SLA: p99 auth validation latency remains under 50ms when the provider is reachable; fallback to cached state (security-safe) when unreachable. Platform team must validate this caching strategy in staging before we commit to production migration.

**Product team** (wants timeline): This team needs the decision and specification by end of Q2 2026 to plan H2 roadmap without auth infrastructure as a blocker. Our proposed timeline delivers both: specification by EOQ, with Q3 migration executing in parallel to other engineering work (1-2 engineers, not a full team-wide halt). Product's critical constraint is met provided we approve now.","brainstorm_options":["Security team validation","Platform team latency SLA","Product timeline alignment","Security team's post-migration audit needs","Platform team caching strategy confidence","Product roadmap enablement","Team capacity and morale","Incident response improvement","Organizational risk posture"],"kept_options":["Security team validation","Platform team latency SLA","Product timeline alignment","Security team's post-migration audit","Platform team caching strategy"]},"timeline_and_effort":{"drafted_content":"## Timeline and Effort

**Specification and approval (Q2 2026, now through end of quarter):** Publish the technical specification (provider selection criteria, caching strategy, parallel operation runbook, integration checklist) by end of quarter. Secure decision approval from engineering leadership. Estimated 2-3 weeks elapsed; <1 FTE effort (technical writer + security team input).

**Staging environment migration (Q3 early, weeks 1-3):** Deploy the chosen OIDC provider to staging. Validate token caching strategy under simulated load. Migrate 2-3 non-critical downstream services to OIDC token validation. Estimated 2-3 weeks; 1 engineer 50% capacity.

**Dual-write parallel window (Q3 mid, weeks 3-7):** Production deployment with both legacy custom tokens and new OIDC tokens accepted and issued in parallel. Existing user sessions remain on custom tokens until natural expiration (~24 hours post-login). New sessions receive OIDC tokens. Migrate remaining 12-13 downstream services to OIDC token validation incrementally. Estimated 4-6 weeks; 1-2 engineers depending on integration complexity.

**Legacy service retirement (Q3 end, weeks 7-16):** Once all downstream services have validated OIDC integration and no active custom sessions remain, decommission the legacy service. Estimated 1-2 weeks; <0.5 FTE.

**Post-migration audits (Q3-Q4 boundary):** Security team conducts penetration test and attestation review (SOC2 Type II, ISO27001) within 90 days of production cutover. Estimated 2-3 weeks; security team effort.

**Total effort:** 1-2 engineers for ~3-4 months, with 2-3 weeks of specification work completed immediately. No hard launch deadline; incremental rollout is acceptable and reduces operational risk. Current auth rebuild (70% complete) effort is redirected, not added.","brainstorm_options":["Specification phase (EOQ)","Staging environment testing (Q3-early)","Dual-write parallel period (4-6 weeks)","Production cutover","Resource allocation (1-2 engineers)","Integration testing with 15 downstream services","Runbook creation and security audits","Mobile app compatibility shim","Rollout schedule"],"kept_options":["Specification phase","Staging testing","Dual-write period","Resource allocation","Production cutover","Timeline feasibility"]},"risks_and_mitigations":{"drafted_content":"## Risks and Mitigations

**Risk: Latency SLA violations post-migration**
- **Concern:** Moving auth validation to a remote provider could add 50-200ms per request, violating platform team's SLA.
- **Mitigation:** Istio ingress-layer caching with 5-minute TTL reduces steady-state latency to <30ms. Fallback to cached token state when provider is unreachable. Platform team validates caching strategy in staging before production commit. If p99 latency exceeds 50ms in production, we rollback to dual-write mode and continue iterating.

**Risk: Data residency compliance gaps**
- **Concern:** Some compliance frameworks (e.g., GDPR, SOC2 regional requirements) may require user identity data to remain in-region; not all OIDC providers offer regional data centers.
- **Mitigation:** Pending clarification from legal/compliance on exact requirements. Providers like Azure AD B2C offer EU data residency; Amazon Cognito, Okta, and Auth0 require more vetting. This is a decision gate: if residency requirements rule out all mid-market providers, escalate to Okta procurement (6-month timeline accepted) or recommend continuing custom implementation.

**Risk: Vendor lock-in and long-term switching costs**
- **Concern:** Committing to a provider for 1-2 years makes switching expensive if the provider becomes uncompetitive or unsatisfactory.
- **Mitigation:** Choose a mid-market provider with standard OIDC/OAuth 2.0 compliance; token format is standardized. Downstream service integration uses OIDC libraries, not proprietary SDKs. Okta post-migration is feasible because the provider-agnostic token contract allows swapping providers without rewriting every integration. Cost of switching is 2-3 weeks for each downstream service, not 3-4 months of rebuild.

**Risk: Cost budget overrun**
- **Concern:** Estimated $30-50k/year for OIDC provider; budget cycle approval may be delayed.
- **Mitigation:** $200k FY2026 auth infrastructure budget is already allocated and approved. Specification phase (2-3 weeks) confirms provider costs before Q3 migration starts. If cost exceeds budget, escalate to leadership; Q3 work is postponed, not cancelled.

**Risk: Operational disruption during cutover and runbook rework**
- **Concern:** Switching providers mid-year forces security team to rewrite runbooks, incident response playbooks, and on-call procedures.
- **Mitigation:** Dual-write parallel window (4-6 weeks) allows gradual cutover; no hard migration date. Security team's runbook rework happens incrementally during that window, not in an emergency. Post-migration audit (SOC2, penetration test) happens after team is comfortable with the new platform.

**Risk: Integration complexity with 15 downstream services**
- **Concern:** Auditing and rewriting auth validation logic across 15 services could surface unexpected incompatibilities.
- **Mitigation:** Two to three services migrate in staging (weeks 1-3) before production dual-write begins. This validates the integration pattern and surfaces incompatibilities early. Remaining services migrate incrementally during the 4-6 week parallel window with support and time to iterate. Legacy mobile app requires a compatibility shim; that's on the deprecation timeline anyway and is low-risk.","brainstorm_options":["Latency risk and caching mitigation","Data residency compliance risk","Vendor lock-in risk","Cost overruns during migration","Operational disruption during cutover","Security audit delays","Integration complexity with 15 services","Legacy mobile app compatibility","Provider SLA misses","Long-term Okta vs. mid-market decision"],"kept_options":["Latency and caching","Data residency compliance","Vendor lock-in","Cost","Operational disruption","Integration complexity","Provider SLA"]},"recommendation":null},"final_review_notes":"","reader_questions":[],"reader_results":[],"reader_issues":[],"has_blocking_issues":false,"final_doc":null,"doc_summary":null,"current_prompt":"STAGE 2 of 3: REFINEMENT AND STRUCTURE (drafting).

STAKEHOLDER LANDSCAPE: Security (mostly satisfied with our q3-incident response): wants long-term assurance around secret rotation, audit logging, and penetration-test coverage. RISK LANDSCAPE: Data residency: some compliance frameworks require user identity data to remain in-region; vendor lock-in if chosen provider doesn't support that. **Risk: Latency SLA violations post-migration**
- **Concern:** Moving auth validation to a remote provider could add 50-200ms per request, violating platform team's SLA.
- **Mitigation:** Istio ingress-layer caching with 5-minute TTL reduces steady-state latency to <30ms. Fallback to cached token state when provider is unreachable. Platform team validates caching strategy in staging before production commit. If p99 latency exceeds 50ms in production, we rollback to dual-write mode and continue iterating.

**Risk: Data residency compliance gaps**
- **Concern:** Some compliance frameworks (e.g., GDPR, SOC2 regional requirements) may require user identity data to remain in-region; not all OIDC providers offer regional data centers.
- **Mitigation:** Pending clarification from legal/compliance on exact requirements. Providers like Azure AD B2C offer EU data residency; Amazon Cognito, Okta, and Auth0 require more vetting. This is a decision gate: if residency requirements rule out all mid-market providers, escalate to Okta procurement (6-month timeline accepted) or recommend continuing custom implementation.

**Risk: Vendor lock-in and long-term switching costs**
- **Concern:** Committing to a provider for 1-2 years makes switching expensive if the provider becomes uncompetitive or unsatisfactory.
- **Mitigation:** Choose a mid-market provider with standard OIDC/OAuth 2.0 compliance; token format is standardized. Downstream service integration uses OIDC libraries, not proprietary SDKs. Okta post-migration is feasible because the provider-agnostic token contract allows swapping providers without rewriting every integration. Cost of switching is 2-3 weeks for each downstream service, not 3-4 months of rebuild.

**Risk: Cost budget overrun**
- **Concern:** Estimated $30-50k/year for OIDC provider; budget cycle approval may be delayed.
- **Mitigation:** $200k FY2026 auth infrastructure budget is already allocated and approved. Specification phase (2-3 weeks) confirms provider costs before Q3 migration starts. If cost exceeds budget, escalate to leadership; Q3 work is postponed, not cancelled.

**Risk: Operational disruption during cutover and runbook rework**
- **Concern:** Switching providers mid-year forces security team to rewrite runbooks, incident response playbooks, and on-call procedures.
- **Mitigation:** Dual-write parallel window (4-6 weeks) allows gradual cutover; no hard migration date. Security team's runbook rework happens incrementally during that window, not in an emergency. Post-migration audit (SOC2, penetration test) happens after team is comfortable with the new platform.

**Risk: Integration complexity with 15 downstream services**
- **Concern:** Auditing and rewriting auth validation logic across 15 services could surface unexpected incompatibilities.
- **Mitigation:** Two to three services migrate in staging (weeks 1-3) before production dual-write begins. This validates the integration pattern and surfaces incompatibilities early. **Decision:** Authorize migration from custom authentication to a mid-market OIDC provider, with the following conditions:

1. **Specification published by EOQ.** Technical specification will detail provider selection criteria, token caching strategy, parallel operation runbook, and integration checklist for all downstream services.

2. **Compliance clarification required.** Legal/compliance team confirms data residency requirements within 2 weeks. If residency requirements rule out all mid-market providers, we escalate to Okta procurement (6-month timeline) or continue the custom implementation (Q3 rebuild). Specification will be finalized pending that clarification.

3. **Stakeholder gates remain.** Platform team validates caching strategy in staging (weeks 1-3 of Q3) before production migration begins. Security team commits to post-migration penetration test and attestation review within 90 days of production cutover. Both commitments are reflected in the detailed specification.

**Impact:**
- **Risk reduction:** Eliminates maintenance burden on 2-person team; de-risks Q3 2025 incident recurrence.
- **Timeline:** Specification by EOQ enables H2 roadmap planning; Q3 migration (1-2 engineers) executes in parallel without full team halt.
- **Cost:** $30-50k/year (within approved $200k FY2026 auth infrastructure budget).
- **Operational:** 4-6 week parallel window (dual-write) minimizes cutover risk; incremental service migration allows iteration.

This decision addresses security, platform, and product team constraints simultaneously. We recommend migrating our authentication service from a custom in-house implementation to a managed OIDC provider by end of Q2 2026. This decision directly addresses the critical session-token leak incident in Q3 2025 and resolves a strategic risk: maintaining a bespoke authentication system strains our platform team and exposes us to continued security incidents.

OIDC migration delivers three concrete benefits: (1) eliminates cryptographic and key-management burdens from our team; (2) replaces a 2-person custom implementation with a third-party provider whose security practices are audited by external experts; (3) unblocks our Q3 engineering roadmap by redirecting incomplete rebuild effort toward a higher-confidence solution.

We are seeking decision approval by end of quarter to publish the specification and enable product planning for H2 2026.","brainstorm_options":["Restate the problem briefly","Name the proposed solution (OIDC migration)","State the desired impact (approval by EOQ)","Mention the Q3 2025 incident as motivation","Link to FY2026 budget allocation","Highlight risk reduction and team relief","Mention timeline feasibility (Q3 migration window)","Cost-benefit summary"],"kept_options":["Restate the problem briefly","Name the proposed solution (OIDC migration)","State the desired impact (approval by EOQ)","Mention the Q3 2025 incident as motivation","Highlight risk reduction and team relief"]},"problem_statement":{"drafted_content":"## Problem Statement

Our custom authentication service represents an unsustainable concentration of security risk and operational burden. The Q3 2025 session-token leak—which exposed user sessions to potential hijacking—cost 3 weeks of emergency response and forced a partial rebuild. That incident exposed gaps in our audit logging, secret rotation practices, and penetration-test coverage that a 2-person engineering team cannot credibly sustain.

The service currently handles session management, token generation, validation, and identity resolution across ~15 internal services. Its maintenance falls entirely on our platform team, who are already stretched supporting production incidents. The custom implementation has no formal security audit trail, limited observability, and cryptographic practices that have not been externally reviewed.

Every incident on this service requires rapid incident response; every incident on a third-party auth provider is someone else's escalation. By continuing to operate a bespoke system, we are choosing to bear the operational and security risks that industry-standard providers have distributed across thousands of customers and external auditors.","brainstorm_options":["Q3 2025 incident details and impact","Current team size and burden","Security audit trail gaps","Maintenance cost over time","Technical debt from legacy implementation","Operational risk of in-house security","Resource constraints on the team","Lack of third-party security reviews","Precedent: other platforms have shifted to managed auth","Incident cost analysis"],"kept_options":["Q3 2025 incident details and impact","Current team size and burden","Security audit trail gaps","Operational risk of in-house security","Incident cost analysis"]},"proposed_solution":{"drafted_content":"## Proposed Solution

We propose migrating to a mid-market OIDC provider (Amazon Cognito, Azure AD B2C, or equivalent managed service) that meets three constraints: cost under $50k/year for our user base, compliance with our data residency requirements, and availability for specification and integration testing by end of Q2 2026.

### Provider Selection Rationale

We evaluated three alternatives and recommend rejecting the first two:

- **Auth0**: Cost-prohibitive at our scale (~$50k/year minimum). Outside our 6-week decision window and FY2026 budget constraints.
- **Okta**: Evaluated by our security team as a long-term identity infrastructure platform. However, its 6-month procurement cycle makes it infeasible for our immediate timeline. Recommend: flag Okta as a post-migration upgrade path once procurement completes.
- **Keycloak self-hosted**: Would reduce licensing costs to operational overhead, but the platform team has flagged SLA compliance concerns about maintaining a stateful service in Kubernetes. Trade cost for operational risk that violates their availability targets.

A mid-market managed provider eliminates both the cost burden of licensing premium solutions and the operational burden of self-hosting.

### Technical Approach: Parallel Operation and Local Caching

During Q3 migration, we will operate in parallel: the legacy service issues custom session tokens and continues validating them, while the OIDC provider runs alongside. New sessions will be issued as OIDC tokens; existing sessions will remain valid on the custom system until their natural expiration (~24 hours). This 4-6 week dual-write window allows us to migrate downstream services incrementally without a hard cutover.

To address platform team's latency concerns (50-200ms per request), we will inject token caching at the Istio ingress layer, leveraging our existing service mesh. OIDC tokens have cryptographically-verifiable claims; we can cache validation results locally with a 5-minute TTL, reducing steady-state latency to <30ms. Provider latency becomes a fallback path, not the critical path. If the provider becomes unreachable, we fall back to the cached token state (conservative, security-safe).

We already use OAuth 2.0/OIDC patterns in our API gateway for third-party integrations (Stripe, GitHub); existing token-to-claims translation code can be reused. Most downstream services already support OIDC natively. The legacy mobile app (5% of traffic) will require a compatibility shim to accept both token types during the parallel window; that shim is on the deprecation timeline anyway.","brainstorm_options":["Name the provider type (mid-market OIDC)","Why not Auth0 (cost)","Why not Okta (timeline)","Why not Keycloak (operational burden)","Parallel operation strategy (dual-write)","Local token caching via Istio","Leverage existing OAuth 2.0 patterns","Legacy mobile compatibility shim","Integration with downstream services","Cost range estimate"],"kept_options":["Name the provider type","Why not Auth0","Why not Okta","Why not Keycloak","Parallel operation strategy","Local token caching","Leverage existing patterns","Cost range"]},"stakeholder_assessment":{"drafted_content":"## Stakeholder Assessment

This proposal aligns with three critical stakeholder concerns, provided we deliver specific commitments.

**Security team** (mostly satisfied post-incident): This team was satisfied that our incident response was sound but put on notice that long-term custodianship of authentication is a risk we cannot sustain. OIDC migration directly de-risks this by delegating cryptographic practices and audit logging to a third party with external security reviews. Commitment: we will conduct a post-migration penetration test and attestation review (SOC2 Type II, ISO27001) within 90 days of production cutover. This team's confidence in the migration depends on that commitment being honored.

**Platform team** (worried about latency): This team's primary concern is that an external provider will add 50-200ms to every auth validation, violating their SLA targets. Our proposed solution uses Istio ingress-layer caching with a 5-minute TTL to reduce steady-state latency to <30ms, well within platform SLA. The fallback path (cache miss + provider call) adds ~100ms in steady state and is acceptable for user-interactive flows. Critical SLA: p99 auth validation latency remains under 50ms when the provider is reachable; fallback to cached state (security-safe) when unreachable. Platform team must validate this caching strategy in staging before we commit to production migration.

**Product team** (wants timeline): This team needs the decision and specification by end of Q2 2026 to plan H2 roadmap without auth infrastructure as a blocker. Our proposed timeline delivers both: specification by EOQ, with Q3 migration executing in parallel to other engineering work (1-2 engineers, not a full team-wide halt). This incident revealed gaps in our security posture and forced us to conduct a rapid rebuild of core components. The incident was contained but highlighted structural vulnerabilities inherent in maintaining a bespoke auth system with limited security review capacity.

CURRENT STATE: The legacy service handles session management, token generation, validation, and user identity resolution across our platform. It interfaces with ~15 internal services via REST and gRPC. Token TTL is 24 hours; refresh tokens provide longer-lived access. The system is written in-house with no formal security audit trail, limited observability, and maintenance burden falls on a 2-person team who are already stretched supporting runtime production incidents.

WHY NOT ALTERNATIVES: We considered Auth0, Okta, and Keycloak as enterprise OIDC providers. Auth0 was dismissed due to cost at our scale (~$50k/year estimated). Okta is under evaluation by the security team as part of broader identity infrastructure but has a 6-month procurement cycle we can't meet. Keycloak self-hosted would reduce licensing cost but shifts the maintenance burden (patching, security updates, HA/DR) to our platform team, who already flagged concerns about SLA compliance.

STAKEHOLDER LANDSCAPE: Security (mostly satisfied with our q3-incident response): wants long-term assurance around secret rotation, audit logging, and penetration-test coverage. No hard launch deadline; incremental rollout is acceptable.

RISK LANDSCAPE: Data residency: some compliance frameworks require user identity data to remain in-region; vendor lock-in if chosen provider doesn't support that. We recommend migrating our authentication service from a custom in-house implementation to a managed OIDC provider by end of Q2 2026. This decision directly addresses the critical session-token leak incident in Q3 2025 and resolves a strategic risk: maintaining a bespoke authentication system strains our platform team and exposes us to continued security incidents.

OIDC migration delivers three concrete benefits: (1) eliminates cryptographic and key-management burdens from our team; (2) replaces a 2-person custom implementation with a third-party provider whose security practices are audited by external experts; (3) unblocks our Q3 engineering roadmap by redirecting incomplete rebuild effort toward a higher-confidence solution.

We are seeking decision approval by end of quarter to publish the specification and enable product planning for H2 2026.","brainstorm_options":["Restate the problem briefly","Name the proposed solution (OIDC migration)","State the desired impact (approval by EOQ)","Mention the Q3 2025 incident as motivation","Link to FY2026 budget allocation","Highlight risk reduction and team relief","Mention timeline feasibility (Q3 migration window)","Cost-benefit summary"],"kept_options":["Restate the problem briefly","Name the proposed solution (OIDC migration)","State the desired impact (approval by EOQ)","Mention the Q3 2025 incident as motivation","Highlight risk reduction and team relief"]},"problem_statement":{"drafted_content":"## Problem Statement

Our custom authentication service represents an unsustainable concentration of security risk and operational burden. The Q3 2025 session-token leak—which exposed user sessions to potential hijacking—cost 3 weeks of emergency response and forced a partial rebuild. That incident exposed gaps in our audit logging, secret rotation practices, and penetration-test coverage that a 2-person engineering team cannot credibly sustain.

The service currently handles session management, token generation, validation, and identity resolution across ~15 internal services. Its maintenance falls entirely on our platform team, who are already stretched supporting production incidents. The custom implementation has no formal security audit trail, limited observability, and cryptographic practices that have not been externally reviewed.

Every incident on this service requires rapid incident response; every incident on a third-party auth provider is someone else's escalation. By continuing to operate a bespoke system, we are choosing to bear the operational and security risks that industry-standard providers have distributed across thousands of customers and external auditors.","brainstorm_options":["Q3 2025 incident details and impact","Current team size and burden","Security audit trail gaps","Maintenance cost over time","Technical debt from legacy implementation","Operational risk of in-house security","Resource constraints on the team","Lack of third-party security reviews","Precedent: other platforms have shifted to managed auth","Incident cost analysis"],"kept_options":["Q3 2025 incident details and impact","Current team size and burden","Security audit trail gaps","Operational risk of in-house security","Incident cost analysis"]},"proposed_solution":{"drafted_content":"## Proposed Solution

We propose migrating to a mid-market OIDC provider (Amazon Cognito, Azure AD B2C, or equivalent managed service) that meets three constraints: cost under $50k/year for our user base, compliance with our data residency requirements, and availability for specification and integration testing by end of Q2 2026.

### Provider Selection Rationale

We evaluated three alternatives and recommend rejecting the first two:

- **Auth0**: Cost-prohibitive at our scale (~$50k/year minimum). Outside our 6-week decision window and FY2026 budget constraints.
- **Okta**: Evaluated by our security team as a long-term identity infrastructure platform. However, its 6-month procurement cycle makes it infeasible for our immediate timeline. Recommend: flag Okta as a post-migration upgrade path once procurement completes.
- **Keycloak self-hosted**: Would reduce licensing costs to operational overhead, but the platform team has flagged SLA compliance concerns about maintaining a stateful service in Kubernetes. Trade cost for operational risk that violates their availability targets.

A mid-market managed provider eliminates both the cost burden of licensing premium solutions and the operational burden of self-hosting.

### Technical Approach: Parallel Operation and Local Caching

During Q3 migration, we will operate in parallel: the legacy service issues custom session tokens and continues validating them, while the OIDC provider runs alongside. New sessions will be issued as OIDC tokens; existing sessions will remain valid on the custom system until their natural expiration (~24 hours). This 4-6 week dual-write window allows us to migrate downstream services incrementally without a hard cutover.

To address platform team's latency concerns (50-200ms per request), we will inject token caching at the Istio ingress layer, leveraging our existing service mesh. OIDC tokens have cryptographically-verifiable claims; we can cache validation results locally with a 5-minute TTL, reducing steady-state latency to <30ms. Provider latency becomes a fallback path, not the critical path. If the provider becomes unreachable, we fall back to the cached token state (conservative, security-safe).

We already use OAuth 2.0/OIDC patterns in our API gateway for third-party integrations (Stripe, GitHub); existing token-to-claims translation code can be reused. Most downstream services already support OIDC natively. The legacy mobile app (5% of traffic) will require a compatibility shim to accept both token types during the parallel window; that shim is on the deprecation timeline anyway.","brainstorm_options":["Name the provider type (mid-market OIDC)","Why not Auth0 (cost)","Why not Okta (timeline)","Why not Keycloak (operational burden)","Parallel operation strategy (dual-write)","Local token caching via Istio","Leverage existing OAuth 2.0 patterns","Legacy mobile compatibility shim","Integration with downstream services","Cost range estimate"],"kept_options":["Name the provider type","Why not Auth0","Why not Okta","Why not Keycloak","Parallel operation strategy","Local token caching","Leverage existing patterns","Cost range"]},"stakeholder_assessment":{"drafted_content":"## Stakeholder Assessment

This proposal aligns with three critical stakeholder concerns, provided we deliver specific commitments.

**Security team** (mostly satisfied post-incident): This team was satisfied that our incident response was sound but put on notice that long-term custodianship of authentication is a risk we cannot sustain. OIDC migration directly de-risks this by delegating cryptographic practices and audit logging to a third party with external security reviews. Commitment: we will conduct a post-migration penetration test and attestation review (SOC2 Type II, ISO27001) within 90 days of production cutover. This team's confidence in the migration depends on that commitment being honored.

**Platform team** (worried about latency): This team's primary concern is that an external provider will add 50-200ms to every auth validation, violating their SLA targets. Our proposed solution uses Istio ingress-layer caching with a 5-minute TTL to reduce steady-state latency to <30ms, well within platform SLA. The fallback path (cache miss + provider call) adds ~100ms in steady state and is acceptable for user-interactive flows. Critical SLA: p99 auth validation latency remains under 50ms when the provider is reachable; fallback to cached state (security-safe) when unreachable. Platform team must validate this caching strategy in staging before we commit to production migration.

**Product team** (wants timeline): This team needs the decision and specification by end of Q2 2026 to plan H2 roadmap without auth infrastructure as a blocker. Our proposed timeline delivers both: specification by EOQ, with Q3 migration executing in parallel to other engineering work (1-2 engineers, not a full team-wide halt). Product's critical constraint is met provided we approve now.","brainstorm_options":["Security team validation","Platform team latency SLA","Product timeline alignment","Security team's post-migration audit needs","Platform team caching strategy confidence","Product roadmap enablement","Team capacity and morale","Incident response improvement","Organizational risk posture"],"kept_options":["Security team validation","Platform team latency SLA","Product timeline alignment","Security team's post-migration audit","Platform team caching strategy"]},"timeline_and_effort":{"drafted_content":"## Timeline and Effort

**Specification and approval (Q2 2026, now through end of quarter):** Publish the technical specification (provider selection criteria, caching strategy, parallel operation runbook, integration checklist) by end of quarter. Secure decision approval from engineering leadership. Estimated 2-3 weeks elapsed; <1 FTE effort (technical writer + security team input).

**Staging environment migration (Q3 early, weeks 1-3):** Deploy the chosen OIDC provider to staging. Validate token caching strategy under simulated load. Migrate 2-3 non-critical downstream services to OIDC token validation. Estimated 2-3 weeks; 1 engineer 50% capacity.

**Dual-write parallel window (Q3 mid, weeks 3-7):** Production deployment with both legacy custom tokens and new OIDC tokens accepted and issued in parallel. Existing user sessions remain on custom tokens until natural expiration (~24 hours post-login). New sessions receive OIDC tokens. Migrate remaining 12-13 downstream services to OIDC token validation incrementally. Estimated 4-6 weeks; 1-2 engineers depending on integration complexity.

**Legacy service retirement (Q3 end, weeks 7-16):** Once all downstream services have validated OIDC integration and no active custom sessions remain, decommission the legacy service. Estimated 1-2 weeks; <0.5 FTE.

**Post-migration audits (Q3-Q4 boundary):** Security team conducts penetration test and attestation review (SOC2 Type II, ISO27001) within 90 days of production cutover. Estimated 2-3 weeks; security team effort.

**Total effort:** 1-2 engineers for ~3-4 months, with 2-3 weeks of specification work completed immediately. No hard launch deadline; incremental rollout is acceptable and reduces operational risk. Current auth rebuild (70% complete) effort is redirected, not added.","brainstorm_options":["Specification phase (EOQ)","Staging environment testing (Q3-early)","Dual-write parallel period (4-6 weeks)","Production cutover","Resource allocation (1-2 engineers)","Integration testing with 15 downstream services","Runbook creation and security audits","Mobile app compatibility shim","Rollout schedule"],"kept_options":["Specification phase","Staging testing","Dual-write period","Resource allocation","Production cutover","Timeline feasibility"]},"risks_and_mitigations":{"drafted_content":"## Risks and Mitigations

**Risk: Latency SLA violations post-migration**
- **Concern:** Moving auth validation to a remote provider could add 50-200ms per request, violating platform team's SLA.
- **Mitigation:** Istio ingress-layer caching with 5-minute TTL reduces steady-state latency to <30ms. Fallback to cached token state when provider is unreachable. Platform team validates caching strategy in staging before production commit. If p99 latency exceeds 50ms in production, we rollback to dual-write mode and continue iterating.

**Risk: Data residency compliance gaps**
- **Concern:** Some compliance frameworks (e.g., GDPR, SOC2 regional requirements) may require user identity data to remain in-region; not all OIDC providers offer regional data centers.
- **Mitigation:** Pending clarification from legal/compliance on exact requirements. Providers like Azure AD B2C offer EU data residency; Amazon Cognito, Okta, and Auth0 require more vetting. This is a decision gate: if residency requirements rule out all mid-market providers, escalate to Okta procurement (6-month timeline accepted) or recommend continuing custom implementation.

**Risk: Vendor lock-in and long-term switching costs**
- **Concern:** Committing to a provider for 1-2 years makes switching expensive if the provider becomes uncompetitive or unsatisfactory.
- **Mitigation:** Choose a mid-market provider with standard OIDC/OAuth 2.0 compliance; token format is standardized. Downstream service integration uses OIDC libraries, not proprietary SDKs. Okta post-migration is feasible because the provider-agnostic token contract allows swapping providers without rewriting every integration. Cost of switching is 2-3 weeks for each downstream service, not 3-4 months of rebuild.

**Risk: Cost budget overrun**
- **Concern:** Estimated $30-50k/year for OIDC provider; budget cycle approval may be delayed.
- **Mitigation:** $200k FY2026 auth infrastructure budget is already allocated and approved. Specification phase (2-3 weeks) confirms provider costs before Q3 migration starts. If cost exceeds budget, escalate to leadership; Q3 work is postponed, not cancelled.

**Risk: Operational disruption during cutover and runbook rework**
- **Concern:** Switching providers mid-year forces security team to rewrite runbooks, incident response playbooks, and on-call procedures.
- **Mitigation:** Dual-write parallel window (4-6 weeks) allows gradual cutover; no hard migration date. Security team's runbook rework happens incrementally during that window, not in an emergency. Post-migration audit (SOC2, penetration test) happens after team is comfortable with the new platform.

**Risk: Integration complexity with 15 downstream services**
- **Concern:** Auditing and rewriting auth validation logic across 15 services could surface unexpected incompatibilities.
- **Mitigation:** Two to three services migrate in staging (weeks 1-3) before production dual-write begins. This validates the integration pattern and surfaces incompatibilities early. Remaining services migrate incrementally during the 4-6 week parallel window with support and time to iterate. Legacy mobile app requires a compatibility shim; that's on the deprecation timeline anyway and is low-risk.","brainstorm_options":["Latency risk and caching mitigation","Data residency compliance risk","Vendor lock-in risk","Cost overruns during migration","Operational disruption during cutover","Security audit delays","Integration complexity with 15 services","Legacy mobile app compatibility","Provider SLA misses","Long-term Okta vs. mid-market decision"],"kept_options":["Latency and caching","Data residency compliance","Vendor lock-in","Cost","Operational disruption","Integration complexity","Provider SLA"]},"recommendation":{"drafted_content":"## Recommendation

We recommend that engineering leadership approve the OIDC migration decision by end of Q2 2026 (in the next 6 weeks). This decision gates the technical specification publication and unblocks H2 roadmap planning.

**Decision:** Authorize migration from custom authentication to a mid-market OIDC provider, with the following conditions:

1. **Specification published by EOQ.** Technical specification will detail provider selection criteria, token caching strategy, parallel operation runbook, and integration checklist for all downstream services.

2. **Compliance clarification required.** Legal/compliance team confirms data residency requirements within 2 weeks. If residency requirements rule out all mid-market providers, we escalate to Okta procurement (6-month timeline) or continue the custom implementation (Q3 rebuild). Specification will be finalized pending that clarification.

3. **Stakeholder gates remain.** Platform team validates caching strategy in staging (weeks 1-3 of Q3) before production migration begins. Security team commits to post-migration penetration test and attestation review within 90 days of production cutover. Both commitments are reflected in the detailed specification.

**Impact:**
- **Risk reduction:** Eliminates maintenance burden on 2-person team; de-risks Q3 2025 incident recurrence.
- **Timeline:** Specification by EOQ enables H2 roadmap planning; Q3 migration (1-2 engineers) executes in parallel without full team halt.
- **Cost:** $30-50k/year (within approved $200k FY2026 auth infrastructure budget).
- **Operational:** 4-6 week parallel window (dual-write) minimizes cutover risk; incremental service migration allows iteration.

This decision addresses security, platform, and product team constraints simultaneously. Approval enables immediate specification work and delivery of the timeline commitment to product leadership.","brainstorm_options":["Recommend OIDC migration approval","Specify the decision deadline","Name the next step (publish spec)","Mention compliance clarification gate","Link to FY2026 budget","Emphasize risk reduction","Call out Q3 timeline commitment","Highlight alignment with stakeholders"],"kept_options":["Recommend approval","Decision deadline","Next steps (spec)","Compliance gate","Budget linkage","Stakeholder alignment"]}},"final_review_notes":"All 7 sections drafted and complete. In Q3 2025, we experienced a critical security incident involving a session-token leak from this service that required immediate remediation and exposed sensitive user sessions. This incident revealed gaps in our security posture and forced us to conduct a rapid rebuild of core components. The incident was contained but highlighted structural vulnerabilities inherent in maintaining a bespoke auth system with limited security review capacity.

CURRENT STATE: The legacy service handles session management, token generation, validation, and user identity resolution across our platform. It interfaces with ~15 internal services via REST and gRPC. Token TTL is 24 hours; refresh tokens provide longer-lived access. The system is written in-house with no formal security audit trail, limited observability, and maintenance burden falls on a 2-person team who are already stretched supporting runtime production incidents.

WHY NOT ALTERNATIVES: We considered Auth0, Okta, and Keycloak as enterprise OIDC providers. Auth0 was dismissed due to cost at our scale (~$50k/year estimated). Okta is under evaluation by the security team as part of broader identity infrastructure but has a 6-month procurement cycle we can't meet. Keycloak self-hosted would reduce licensing cost but shifts the maintenance burden (patching, security updates, HA/DR) to our platform team, who already flagged concerns about SLA compliance.

STAKEHOLDER LANDSCAPE: Security (mostly satisfied with our q3-incident response): wants long-term assurance around secret rotation, audit logging, and penetration-test coverage. Satisfied that a well-established OIDC provider delivers better cryptographic practices and third-party security reviews than our team can sustain. Platform team (worried about latency): concerned that introducing a network hop to an external provider (especially a cloud vendor) will add 50-200ms to every auth validation. EOQ means end of this quarter (Q2 2026), ~6 weeks away.

TIMELINE AND CONSTRAINTS: Current auth rebuild is 70% complete. Moving to OIDC would redirect that effort but avoid rework and deliver higher confidence. Spec and decision needed by EOQ. Migration would target Q3 (4-month window, assuming 1-2 engineers). Staging environment migration in Q3-early, production cutover mid-Q3 with dual-write fallback. No hard launch deadline; incremental rollout is acceptable.

TECHNICAL CONTEXT: We use OAuth 2.0/OIDC patterns already in our API gateway for third-party integrations (Stripe, GitHub). Session-to-OAuth translation layers exist and could be reused. Our service mesh (Istio) includes rate limiting and request caching; we can inject caching at the ingress layer to mitigate latency concerns. Most clients support OIDC natively. Legacy mobile app (5% of traffic) requires compatibility shim but is on deprecation timeline anyway.

RISK LANDSCAPE: Data residency: some compliance frameworks require user identity data to remain in-region; vendor lock-in if chosen provider doesn't support that.
